data.title: Drivers could be fined years after speeding
data.description:
Drivers face being fined years after breaking the speed limit under Labour’s road safety plans.

Ministers are planning to remove the six-month time limit on legal action for speeding offences, which has resulted in thousands of cases being cancelled.

Under the plans, the six-month time limit will only start when police have been informed who was driving a vehicle that broke the speed limit or went through a red light, which can sometimes take months.

The Government argues that the requirement for alleged offenders to receive a court summons within six months is “open to abuse and delaying tactics” and “can result in offenders avoiding justice”.

If a driver contests a fine, some alleged offenders can wait 18 months for a magistrates’ hearing because of court backlogs.

But Nick Freeman, one of Britain’s top motoring lawyers, told The Telegraph that the proposed new system would only result in further delays, and that some motorists accused of speeding could wait years for the result of legal action.

He said the Government’s plan was a “lame excuse to cover for their own inefficiencies”, arguing that “the only people abusing this system are the prosecuting authorities”.

“There has to be a time limitation,” he said. “The purpose of this legislation is so that people can genuinely remember who was driving the vehicle, but the problem is the authorities are not efficient. They need to get their act together.”

Mr Freeman, who runs his own legal firm and is known as “Mr Loophole” for his success in defending celebrities convicted of driving offences, said further delays would result in slower justice for innocent people.

“We don’t want people to be prosecuted for a red light offence three years later,” he said.

The current system requires police to issue a suspect with a Section 172 notice, which requires them to confirm who was driving the vehicle at the time of the alleged offence.

Within 28 days, the owner of the vehicle must then confirm that they were driving at the time, or give police details of the person who was behind the wheel. The second person is then given a further 28 days to respond.

After receiving the information, police are then required to ask a magistrates’ court to issue a summons. All stages of the process must be completed within six months for the prosecution to continue, and courts often issue a summons on the final day of that period.

The Government does not routinely publish data on the number of speeding prosecutions abandoned by prosecutors, although analysis by the RAC Foundation in 2022 found that 17 per cent were scrapped for a variety of reasons, including that prosecutors exceeded the six-month limit.

Speeding offences are usually punished with a fine as a percentage of a driver’s weekly income, up to a maximum of £1,000. Offenders can also be ordered to participate in speed awareness courses.

Labour’s road safety plan also includes a plan to give people who drive without wearing a seatbelt three points on their driving licence, in addition to the current penalty of a fine. The rule will also apply to passengers under the age of 14, who are legally the responsibility of the driver.

Ministers have already been criticised for the plan to lower drink-driving limits, which hospitality bosses said will reduce footfall in rural pubs.

The Telegraph has launched a campaign to save Britain’s pubs after a series of measures by Labour left thousands of businesses questioning whether they would be able to stay afloat.

In response, Lillian Greenwood, the road safety minister, said the new rules were not designed to prevent the public “having a great night out”.

“What we’re just saying is don’t take your car,” she told Times Radio. “So that might mean that, you know, some places you’ll be able to take a bus or a taxi. In other places, you’re going out with a group of mates, one of you agrees to be the designated driver.”

A Department for Transport spokeswoman said: “This consultation will seek all views before any decisions are made.”

data.title: Billionaire former Tory donor pays himself £477m
data.description:
A billionaire former Tory donor paid himself £477m last year as revenues soared at his City hedge fund.

Chris Rokos cemented his position as one of Britain’s richest men, and is believed to be the highest-paid partner at his eponymous fund, Rokos Capital Management, which more than doubled its turnover from £445m to £1.2bn.

The fund’s latest accounts revealed that the total pool of profits for its 23 partners rose to a record £940m in the 12 months to March, up from £315m the year prior.

While the results do not disclose the identity of the highest-paid partner, Mr Rokos is widely considered the fund’s top earner, who founded the firm in 2015 and is its majority owner.

Mr Rokos, who is one of Britain’s leading hedge fund managers, was also previously a donor to the Conservatives, giving the party around £2m between 2009 and 2015. He made his last donation in 2018.

He also owns the centuries-old, Grade I listed Tottenham House, one of England’s grandest stately homes. Earlier this year, Mr Rokos made headlines by applying for planning permission to install around 1,100 solar panels to provide power to the 100-bed mansion.

Under the plans, two acres of the manor estate – currently used for silage and grazing dairy cows – would be developed to house solar panels. These would provide nearly one third of the power required to run the estate.

Tottenham House was part of the Savernake Estate, once a royal hunting forest with significant links to Henry VIII. The family of Jane Seymour, the King’s third wife, took ownership of the home before passing it down through several generations.

Mr Rokos has been working to restore the house into a family home.

He has launched a series of ambitious construction projects, such as underground tunnels connecting the main house to the stables. He has also installed a tennis pavilion and a cinema.

Before launching Rokos Capital, he also co-founded Brevan Howard, another City hedge fund.

Unlike most large hedge funds, which have multiple portfolio managers making bets on the market, Mr Rokos is the main trader at Rokos Capital.

He is supported by a team of analysts and senior investment professionals, which the firm calls investment officers.

Rokos Capital Management was contacted for comment.

data.title: Shutting down Britain because of bad weather is pathetic
data.description:
When we touched down at Heathrow on Saturday, and the pilot warned of “a big freeze” in the UK – with temperatures “sinking as low as -3C” – my daughter and I fell about laughing. It had been -16C in New York, and the wind blasts were such that you felt the cold not just in your bones, but your entrails – such that you could never imagine feeling warm again.

Did anybody (but us) mention the weather? Nope. In their duvet coats, balaclavas and snow boots, hardy New Yorkers somehow got on with their lives. They took buses and trains that somehow worked, despite inches of snow. They made it to work on time and did the jobs they were paid to do. With what little emotion my frozen limbic system was still able to produce, I felt awed by their grit – by the collective functionality of the Empire City.

Can you imagine any visitor saying that about us? Not if the Trades Unions Congress (TUC) has anything to do with it. We’re not yet a week into 2026, and already, the TUC has embarked on a concerted campaign to “Stop Britain Working” – and by “working”, they also mean functioning.

There’s the light dusting of snow, for starters, which has instantly prompted the usual hilariously overblown rhetoric. No one should be “pressured into making dangerous journeys”, the TUC warned on Sunday, and workers “must not” be penalised if they cannot get to work. 

When “snow causes problems on the UK’s transport network,” it had previously said, “it makes sense for employers to encourage and enable staff to work from home if they can, rather than struggle with a lengthy, risky and difficult commute to and from work”.

Yes, we can’t have any struggling. And it goes without saying that nothing in professional life should ever be either lengthy or difficult. Grit is something to be scattered on icy roads (if the scatterers can be bothered), not a quality to aspire to. Although it should perhaps be pointed out that the Met Office advice was slightly more sanguine. People, it said yesterday, should “plan to leave the house at least five minutes earlier than normal”.

Once the Arctic freeze and the “travel chaos” are over, and once we’re done talking about just how Arctic and chaotic it really was, the TUC would like us to focus on a more enduring component of their “Stop Britain Working” campaign – and that is, of course, “stress”. That little gem of a word – the opportunist’s gift that keeps on giving.

You may not be aware of this, but we are, in fact, in the middle of a “national work-related stress crisis”, and the unions are demanding urgent intervention. In a recent TUC survey of 2,700 safety representatives, four out of five reported stress as a primary concern, with many respondents claiming that “excessive workloads” are driving that stress to unprecedented levels.

TUC general secretary, Paul Nowak, warned on Sunday that: “These findings expose a growing national crisis. Stress is now entrenched as the biggest health and safety issue facing working people, and the situation is getting worse.” 

He added: “No worker should find themselves lying awake at night from stress, but too many employers are ignoring the law, failing to assess stress risks, and piling impossible workloads on to staff. Workers are burning out, and they are paying with their health.”

“Getting worse” – really? Are we genuinely saying that we are more stressed and overburdened in our working lives than any previous generation? Because that’s quite a bold claim. One that – I think most of us can agree – isn’t supported by external evidence.

I understand that stress was and is a real thing (that has since been hijacked by the work-shy). I understand that it is bad for us and that we should try and avoid it, wherever possible. But the idea that we somehow have it worse than ever before? 

The notion that what Britain needs right now is to work less hard, to cut itself some slack, to take a few more duvet days and indulge in some wellness journaling on the couch? I don’t actually have a comeback for that.

I will, however, say this. I don’t believe that we are an inherently feeble country. I know we’re not. Which is what makes this assumption of feebleness 10 times worse, because assumed feebleness creates true feebleness, and when you look at the rash of headlines over the first week of the year – “One in Ten British Workers Wants To Resign This Month”, “No Point in Working Harder: The Rise of Part-time Britain” – it’s not terribly heartening.

Do we really want feebleness to become our unique selling point? Because that really would be a national crisis.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: 5G rollout blamed for scrambling aircraft altitude readings
data.description:
Pilots are struggling to accurately determine their aircraft altitude due to interference from 5G signals.

Airlines have warned that 5G communications networks are affecting the radio altimeters that aircrew rely on to determine an aircraft’s altitude.

The International Air Transport Association (IATA) said that existing filters to remove 5G interference are only partially effective, warning that a new system may not be ready until 2035.

While pilots have other ways of determining their altitude, the loss of the radio altimeter – which bounces radio signals off the ground – poses a safety risk in certain phases of flight.

Nick Careen, IATA’s safety chief, said interference is most prevalent at lower altitudes in urban areas. That makes aircraft vulnerable as they approach landing and has forced some to abort landings at the last minute.

Mr Careen said: “The biggest area of concern is around the airport. Altitude can be confirmed in other ways, but if it happens on final approach, you will more than likely have to pull out of the landing and reset.”

He added that interference has already led to several “go-around” incidents, in which aircraft pull up rather than land.

While older 4G phone signals did not interfere with aircraft communications, the switch to 5G has brought the two closer together in the radio spectrum, increasing the risk of interference through a process known as “bleeding”.

‘Problem for the next 10 years’

Radio altimeters use the 4.2 GHz to 4.4 GHz frequency band, while the 5G C-band operates just below that range, from 3.4 GHz to 4.2 GHz.

Mr Careen said the situation could be eased by “telecoms companies making sure that they have their towers pointing in a direction away from the airport and lowering the frequency so it’s not right up against the frequency we are operating in”.

However, given the scale of the 5G rollout, with spectrum rights sales in the US alone raising $100bn (£80bn) for the federal government, the airline industry has little comparative clout.

He said: “It’s a difficult argument for us to make, given the size of this market. From a government perspective, you’d say, go and put more filters in your aircraft.”

The situation was exacerbated by a dispute over radio-frequency allocation between the Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) and the Federal Communications Commission in the US.

The pair eventually agreed to a compromise deal that runs until 2028, but not before the FAA issued warnings about potential 5G interference with a number of planes, including the Boeing 737.

A new generation of radio filtering technology has the potential to resolve the interference issue once and for all. Still, it is unlikely to be ready before 2032 or even 2035, according to Mr Careen.

He said: “We have got a problem for the next 10 years or so, and we need to ensure that 5G is deployed safely.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: US ‘prediction market’ gambling boom hits Britain
data.description:
America’s “prediction market” betting craze is landing in Britain as a gambling group gears up to launch the first exhange of its kind in the UK.

Matchbook, a popular sports betting group, is expected to roll out the UK’s first prediction market in the coming weeks, allowing Britons to make yes or no bets against each other on sporting and political events.

Prediction markets have boomed in the US with $44bn (£32.8bn) staked by gamblers last year. The sector is dominated by the world’s two largest exchanges, Kalshi and Polymarket, neither of which are available in the UK.

Unlike traditional betting platforms, prediction markets offer peer-to-peer stakes that allow gamblers to bet against each other with the exchange operators taking a small commission fee for each bet.

Matchbook hopes to emulate Kalshi and Polymarket’s success and usher in a similar boom to Britain.

Ronan McDonagh, the group’s chief executive, said that the company’s original intention was to launch a prediction market in the US but it decided to release a version in the UK while it waits for an American licence.

“We spent most of the last year working on it,” Mr McDonagh said. “We did the application in October but obviously the government shutdown delayed everything.

“An exchange and a prediction market are fundamentally the same thing, it’s just the layout and there are some different nuances to it ... So for us to move from an exchange to a prediction market is not a big leap.”

Bets can be wide-ranging and esoteric, from whether Spain will win the World Cup to whether Jesus Christ will return in 2026.

More than $3.6bn was staked at Polymarket on the outcome of the 2024 US presidential race.

A mystery trader last week netted more than $400,000 by successfully betting on the timing of Nicolás Maduro’s capture on Polymarket.

Matchbook will limit bets on current affairs events because prediction markets in the UK are considered gambling products, which don’t allow the more exotic forecasts popular with US rivals.

Betting exchanges, such as the one Matchbook already operates in the UK, already operate similarly to prediction markets with the key differences largely being in presentation.

As such, Matchbook already has the required licenses to operate a prediction market in the UK and much of the technical know-how.

The US considers prediction markets to be financial derivatives, rather than gambling products, making them easier to access and circumventing America’s notoriously strict gambling laws.

Matchbook was at one point controlled by a group of investors including Matthew Benham, the owner of Brentford FC.

It is now majority-owned by Zeljko Ranogajec, a professional gambler originally from Australia. It is registered in Guernsey and has been operating a sports exchange for 20 years.

The company said it was undergoing the review process for its licence with US regulators and was in discussion with a number of potential marketing and distribution partners in the US.

Matchbook is also providing a white label prediction market for easyBet, part of easyJet founder Sir Stelios Haji-Ioannou’s group, which is expected to launch in February. EasyBet was contacted for comment.

The launch of Matchbook’s prediction market is expected to happen in January, possibly as early as next week.

data.title: Hong Kong billionaire plots London listing for Superdrug
data.description:
Hong Kong’s richest man is plotting a London float for the owner of Superdrug in a boost for Britain’s beleaguered stock exchange.

Sir Li Ka-shing, who has a £43bn fortune, has hired Goldman Sachs and UBS to work on the initial public offering (IPO) of the health and beauty chain’s owner, AS Watson, which has owned the group for two decades.

Under the plan, AS Watson shares would be listed in London and Hong Kong, where Sir Ka-shing has the majority of his business interests through his CK Hutchison conglomerate.

Superdrug is Britain’s second-largest health and beauty retailer behind Boots, boasting sales of £1.6bn last year.

Alongside the chain, AS Watson also owns The Perfume Shop and dozens of other brands, operating 17,000 stores across more than 30 markets worldwide. A float by CK Hutchison could take place this year and raise $2bn (£1.48bn) of proceeds, Bloomberg reported.

Sir Ka-shing is best known for his sprawling British business empire, with CK Hutchison owning key water and power infrastructure across the UK.

The group controls power companies Wales & West Utilities, Northern Gas Networks, Northumbrian Water Group and UK Power Networks, which supplies over eight million customers.

It also runs Eversholt Rail, Britain’s largest owner of railway rolling stock, owns 49pc of mobile network VodafoneThree and has been linked to a takeover of Thames Water.

Last year, CK Hutchison sold the Panama Canal ports to US giant BlackRock after Donald Trump intensified pressure, raising concerns about Chinese influence in the region.

The deal included 43 ports across 23 countries, including the two canal terminals, which are of major strategic importance for trade between the Atlantic and Pacific oceans.

Sir Ka-shing, 97, retired as chairman of CK in 2018 but remains a senior advisor to the group, which employs 300,000 people globally.

The development of Superdrug

Now a staple of the British high street, Superdrug was founded by the Goldstein brothers, Peter and Ronald, in 1964. The first shop opened in April in Putney, west London, and still operates today.

The beauty and health retailer was sold to Woolworth’s parent, Kingfisher, for £57m in 1987. It was later acquired by Kruidvat Group and, shortly after, by AS Watson.

The float of Superdrug’s owner would be a boost for the London Stock Exchange after a dearth of deals.

Concerns reached fever pitch last year after an anticipated revival fell flat and several companies, including UK fintech Wise, announced plans to switch their main listings to the US.

There were just 22 London floats last year, including nine listings on the main exchange, raising only £2.2bn.

AS Watson was contacted for comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Barclay brothers hit with HSBC bankruptcy petition
data.description:
The Barclay brothers are facing the threat of bankruptcy as HSBC seeks to recover more than £140m from the pair.

The bank filed a bankruptcy petition against Aidan Barclay, 69, and Howard Barclay, 66, in the High Court just days before Christmas as it seeks to recover money owed by their failed parcel delivery venture.

The decision to pursue the siblings personally marks an escalation of efforts by creditors to retrieve debts as the Barclays’ business empire unravels. The ongoing collapse was triggered by Lloyds Bank’s decision to seize The Telegraph and The Spectator from the Barclay family in 2023 over unpaid debts.

At the height of their powers, the Barclay family had amassed a vast collection of business interests spanning media, hotels, retail, property and logistics.

However, their dealmaking relied heavily on debt and the family have been forced to surrender control of the majority of their assets after a succession of lenders caught up with them.

HSBC is seeking to minimise its losses from the collapse of Logistics Group in 2024. It was the parent company of Yodel and Arrow XL, two parcel delivery businesses.

The bank has managed to recoup just £1.1m of the £143.5m it was owed when Logistics Group was forced to call in the administrators – equivalent to just 0.78p in the pound – according to a report by insolvency practitioners Teneo filed at Companies House.

It is thought the HSBC proceedings have no bearing on The Telegraph because the Barclays no longer have an economic interest in the newspaper.

The Barclay family sold their interest in the newspaper to RedBird IMI, which is majority-funded by the UAE.

A plan to take control of The Telegraph ultimately failed after the Government introduced new laws banning state ownership of UK newspapers.

Business empire unravels

The Gulf backers of that deal are now also pursuing the Barclays after helping the family to pay off debts to Lloyds Banking Group in 2023.

International Media Investments, which provided funds to RedBird IMI, seized control of the Barclays’ property empire, Trenport Property Holdings, last month.

Meanwhile, The Daily Mail has submitted a £500m bid to buy The Telegraph, which is currently being reviewed by Lisa Nandy, the Culture Secretary.

Further debt pressures led to the Barclay family giving up Yodel in 2024, which it sold for £1. The Barclays also sold off The Spectator magazine’s headquarters to a Bavarian rubber glove tycoon and parted with their prized super-yacht the Lady Beatrice, which was named after the brothers’ late mother.

The Barclays have also been forced to relinquish ownership of Very Group, the online retailer, which owns the Littlewoods brand.

US private equity firm Carlyle took over the company – regarded as the jewel in crown of the Barclays’ sprawling empire – in November, having kept the business afloat over the past year with a series of high-interest loans.

Very was formed 20 years ago through the merger of Littlewoods and Shop Direct, overseen by Aidan and Howard’s late father Sir David Barclay and his twin brother Sir Frederick.

The unwinding of the family’s business interests has prompted questions about their stewardship.

The Telegraph revealed a £278m black hole in its finances in 2024, as a result of loans extracted by the Barclays, which are regarded as unrecoverable.

The sum, recorded as a “provision” in accounts for 2023, pushed The Telegraph to a record loss of £245m that year.

Attempts to trace the missing money via a network of companies controlled by the Barclay family have been unsuccessful.

Specialists from HMRC and the National Crime Agency, which acts as an information clearing house for financial authorities including the Serious Fraud Office, have been in contact with the company and its advisers.

It has not been disclosed if investigations have been launched.

Under the Barclays, The Telegraph was also accused of suppressing negative stories about HSBC. Peter Oborne resigned as the paper’s chief political commentator in 2015, saying his own investigations into the bank had been rejected and claiming there had been a “collapse in standards” since Sir David and Sir Frederick Barclay bought the paper in 2004.

The Barclays and HSBC declined to comment.

data.title: Starmer’s rail nationalisation risks returning Britain to 1970s, warns Labour MP
data.description:
Sir Keir Starmer’s blueprint for nationalising the railway risks dragging the network back to the 1970s, one of his own MPs has warned.

Scott Arthur, a member of the Commons transport select committee, said that making Heidi Alexander, the Transport Secretary, the ultimate arbiter in disputes concerning Great British Railways (GBR), could politicise the most minor decisions.

He said that would prompt scrutiny on a par with British Rail (BR) in the 1970s, when even the state of its notoriously unpalatable sandwiches became the subject of political debate.

Mr Arthur, the Labour MP for Edinburgh South West, said at a meeting of the select committee: “For GBR to succeed it has to have some independence from the Government, it has to be at arm’s length.

“With the Secretary of State having so much discretion and so much input over it, is there not a danger that it’s just going to become politicised?

“We’re going to end up having urgent statements on how fresh the sandwiches are on the railway like we used to do back in the old days.”

Sandwiches sold on British Rail services in the 1970s, often served soggy after being bound in cling film, were a political football and staple of comedy shows for decades.

The sandwiches became a source of frustration for British Rail, generating more headlines than innovations such as the High Speed Train.

Prue Leith was hired to oversee an overhaul in 1977, but the sandwiches still featured prominently in a privatisation debate a decade later, with then Tory MP Teresa Gorman calling for one to be preserved as a symbol of the state-run railway.

Mr Arthur told MPs on Wednesday that handing powers currently held by the Office of Rail and Road to the Transport Secretary also risked undermining public confidence in nationalisation.

Rail-freight firms and the handful of private passenger operators that will carry on providing so-called open access services have claimed that GBR will inevitably favour its own services when allocating paths for trains, and that Ms Alexander is likely to back its decisions.

Mr Arthur said: “The Secretary of State is accumulating all these powers. As a member of the public, there’s no transparency there if I’m concerned about the decision-making process.

“There’s no way for me to understand how decisions are reached if it’s the Secretary of State that’s guiding the process and she’s one of the partners within the process.”

He said that while some people might be “excited to hear the ORR will be monitoring the situation,” the public would prefer it to have “more teeth.”

Mr Arthur said he was also concerned that Ms Alexander would be able slash targets for boosting freight operations at her own discretion, and questioned why the railways bill currently passing through Parliament includes no targets for passenger numbers.

He said: “Is there not going to be a temptation that to reduce the cost to the taxpayer they simply reduce services and end up with fewer passengers on the railway?”

Lord Hendy, the rail minister, said the idea that the Government would become more hands on following nationalisation was “fatuous” and that once train and track operations were united under GBR, he intended to be less closely involved with day-to-day matters.

He told the committee: “I don’t want to answer these letters about why the 8.24am from Richmond didn’t run this morning. I want them to be answered by someone who is actually responsible.

“The people running GBR will have nowhere to go because GBR will be responsible for everything. I want them to wake up and say, ‘This is all my fault and we have got to fix it.’”

He said the railway had even proved “incapable” of implementing a new timetable on the line from London Kings Cross to Edinburgh despite £4bn of investment, forcing him to take on a duty that would fall to a minister nowhere else “outside North Korea and the old USSR”.

data.title: BT could face investigation after digital switch leaves customers without phone line
data.description:
Are you a BT customer experiencing problems because of the digital switchover? Tell us about your experience at money@telegraph.co.uk*

BT could face an investigation after its digital rollout left some vulnerable customers without access to a phone line.

Regulator Ofcom said it could investigate and potentially fine the company if bungled landline cut-offs put customers at risk.

The telecoms giant is currently rolling out “Digital Voice”, a new system that will scrap traditional copper landlines and make all phone lines digital by 2027.

However, the provider has been accused of a botched rollout after people were left without any connectivity over Christmas. Pensioners and vulnerable residents in rural communities risked being left without access to emergency services, and businesses were left without an income.

Ofcom requires providers to have policies in place to ensure vulnerable customers are treated fairly, and says the ability to contact emergency services is a “critically important function” of networks.

Tony Farrar, 78, from Nottinghamshire, said his relatives were unable to wish him a merry Christmas after his landline was disconnected in December.

BT reassured Mr Farrar there would be no change to his phone number or contract. Days later, he received a new contract in the post, and was soon notified his number would change.

When his Digital Voice was installed on Dec 16, Mr Farrar’s connection was cut off altogether.

The retiree was told his connection would return on Jan 2 but he was not reconnected until Jan 6.

“I have spent hours on the telephone since October over this. The fact that they were just ignoring it was beyond belief,” Mr Farrar said.

“This time of year I have family, friends calling on the landline. They couldn’t get through, we were not getting those calls.

“I’m fairly compos mentis, but I am 78 and I know many people my age who wouldn’t have a cat in hell’s chance of dealing with this situation. It’s not fair on people, particularly when it’s not something they’re asking to be done.”

MPs have raised concerns that the Digital Voice rollout could leave rural customers without access to emergency services in the event of a blackout.

Mr Farrar added: “There’s going to be a lot of older people who struggle with this. They can’t make 999 calls and they’ve been messed about.”

A BT spokesman apologised and said the company was in touch with Mr Farrar to restore his connection as quickly as possible.

Joe Goodhart, 90, from East Yorkshire, couldn’t reach a family member in a difficult circumstance over Christmas because his landline with third-party reseller Love Business Group for business broadband – which is provided by BT – was cut off. BT told The Telegraph that it no longer manages Mr Goodhart’s phone line and therefore could not reconnect their landline.

Mr Goodhart said: “The telephone was just cut off one day – we picked up the phone to use it and it was dead. That was six weeks ago and the phone is still cut off now. It has interfered with our family.”

The crisis puts BT at risk of investigation by Ofcom, which fined Virgin Media £23.8m in December when vulnerable customers were put at risk by a bungled digital landline switchover.

The Virgin Media blackout meant customers with so-called “telecare” devices – personal alarms designed to be activated in a health emergency or after a fall – were unable to contact emergency services.

An Ofcom spokesman said: “We have clear rules and guidance that phone companies must follow when switching customers to digital landlines. Providers must minimise disruption, communicate effectively with customers, and support people who might be vulnerable. If we see evidence of widespread issues, we’ve shown we’ll step in.”

“Earlier in December, we fined Virgin Media £23.8m for putting vulnerable customers at risk of harm during its programme to migrate customers to digital landlines.”

A report published by the Digital Communities parliamentary group in March warned migrations could have “deeply personal repercussions” on vulnerable older and disabled people.

Anna Sabine, the group’s Lib Dem member, told The Telegraph: “It is disgraceful and dangerous that vulnerable people were left without access over Christmas.

“We warned that the safety net wasn’t there, and it is unacceptable that retirees and families in remote areas have paid the price for this botched rollout during the most isolating time of the year.”

Matt Barker, 58 from Oxfordshire, opted to move from landline to fibre connection in August when BT indicated it would reduce the couple’s monthly bill. They said they lost months of business at their stained glass restoration company when BT cut off their landline, meaning customers who called their business number couldn’t get through.

Mr Barker said: “The engineer [who installed fibre connection] assured us the landline, which we use as our work phone, would be working by the end of the day.

“But it never worked. We rang BT, we sent several emails – it took about 12 weeks. When they finally sent an engineer out, he said he only worked with the old copper system and couldn’t help us.

“I got a message out of the blue in mid December saying Digital Voice was now connected, and the phone worked – but it really should have happened the day they changed us over.

“We know we missed business. We had someone on our mobile saying they’d been trying to get hold of us for weeks and weeks on our landline. And I wasted hours of time.

“I’m in my late 50s, I’m not really au fait with tech, but my daughters are and they couldn’t understand it either.”

A BT spokesman said, “We’ve investigated Mr Barker’s complaint, and we can confirm that Mr Barker requested an upgrade with us and this request was completed. Mr Barker hasn’t contacted us to report a loss of service, which we’d be more than happy to support with. We are trying to reach Mr Barker to resolve his complaint.”

The crisis could also spell legal trouble for BT, a retail law expert has warned.

Dean Dunham, a consumer lawyer, said: “Whilst BT is entitled to carry out the switch from analogue landlines to Digital Voice, a change which has been signalled for years and is happening across the industry, this does not give it a free pass to leave customers without a working phone service for weeks or months.

“If people are paying for a landline and it isn’t working, particularly over an extended period, BT risks being in breach of contract.

“There are also heightened obligations when it comes to pensioners, vulnerable customers and anyone who relies on a landline for safety or emergency contact. Ofcom rules are clear that these customers must be identified, supported and protected during the switchover.

“Where vulnerable people have been left cut off over Christmas, that raises serious questions about whether those obligations have been properly met.”

A BT spokesman said it has been raising awareness of the switchover for the past two years.

A statement said: “We’ve reviewed the customer cases shared with us. Our investigation indicates delays in Mr Farrah’s and Mr Barker’s switchovers were linked to number transfer and setup issues, some involving other suppliers. We’re working with both customers to resolve these issues. Mr Goodhart’s enquiry relates to a business phone line service provided by a third party.

“We continue to encourage our customers experiencing issues to contact us directly so we can review their setup and provide the best solution for their needs.

“For customers with additional needs, we offer free battery back-up units, hybrid phones and in-home assistance. We’re also investing in improving mobile network resilience in rural areas.

“Anyone with questions or concerns about the switchover should contact their landline provider, who can make sure they have the right solutions in place.”

Two in five UK adults have a landline at home, with a quarter of households using it regularly, a GoCompare survey conducted via YouGov found in February last year.

Catherine Hiley, a broadband expert at GoCompare said: “Not everyone in the UK has home broadband. And digital phones may stop working during a power cut unless there’s a backup solution.

“Landline providers should contact customers ahead of the switchover to explain the process and any steps needed to keep services running smoothly.”

*Please note that by submitting your content to us, you are consenting to The Telegraph processing your personal data where required by law. For further details, please see our Privacy Notice.

data.title: Google billionaire flees California as wealth tax looms
data.description:
Sergey Brin, the billionaire co-founder of Google, has fled California amid an exodus of the state’s richest citizens over fears they will be hit by a wealth tax.

Mr Brin moved more than a dozen entities based in California out of the state in the final days of last year, according to documents obtained by The New York Times.

The move comes days after it emerged that Larry Page, his co-founder, had also left the state.

Mr Brin, 52, is the world’s fourth richest person thanks to his 6pc stake in Alphabet, Google’s parent company. He is worth an estimated $259bn (£193bn).

Momentum is building in California for a 5pc one-off wealth tax on anyone with a net worth of more than $1bn. This would cost Mr Brin around $13bn.

A healthcare union is gathering signatures for a referendum on the tax in November. While it is unclear whether the policy will go to the polls or if voters would approve it, the tax would apply to anyone living in California from the start of this year.

Gavin Newsom, the Democratic governor of California, has said he is opposed to a wealth tax.

Fears of the raid have led to a scramble by wealthy residents to leave the state.

Mr Brin has moved several of his business interests and organisations, including one that manages one of his superyachts, to Nevada in recent days.

He is reportedly house hunting in Miami, which has been a favourite destination for tech billionaires fleeing California.

Mr Page has bought two Miami mansions, it emerged earlier this week. He spent a combined $173.4m on the properties in the exclusive beachfront Coconut Grove neighbourhood.

Mr Page and Mr Brin founded Google in 1998 and ran the company until 2015 when they created Alphabet.

Mr Brin officially stepped down in 2019. However, two years ago, he returned to the company to help with its artificial intelligence (AI) efforts amid concerns it was losing ground to rivals such as ChatGPT maker OpenAI.

The duo’s exit means none of the world’s five richest people live in California despite four of them founding their companies there. Elon Musk, the world’s richest man, and Larry Ellison, the fifth richest, no longer reside in the state. Jeff Bezos, the world’s third richest man, set up Amazon in Seattle but lives primarily in Florida.

Mark Zuckerberg, the world’s sixth richest person with a fortune of $229bn, continues to live in California.

This week Jensen Huang, the billionaire founder of Nvidia, said he was relaxed about the prospect of a tax.

data.title: The middle-class battle to hire (and keep) domestic help
data.description:
Emma Horton has been “ghosted” by two cleaners in the past year.

“One seemed really keen, came a couple of times but then never returned and stopped responding to messages,” says Horton, 48, who lives in Cambridgeshire and owns a PR company. “Another did a similar thing, while the third kept cancelling at the last minute for various reasons, each one more far-fetched than the last, so I let her go. I’ve given up trying to find one.”

Call it a first-world problem but finding someone to clean your house seems more difficult than ever these days.

Several times a week, my local WhatsApp group, which numbers almost 650 people across Essex and Suffolk, pings with a variation on the same message: “Help! Can anyone recommend a good cleaner?”

It’s not only cleaners. Sourcing gardeners is a problem, too – just ask the King. Staff shortages, low pay and the monarch’s exacting demands have reportedly led to an exodus from Highgrove House, his Gloucestershire residence. Since 2022, 11 out of 12 garden staff have left, including two head gardeners.

And then there’s the battle for builders and tradesmen. Often, people report trying to get quotes from as many as seven or eight builders – in many cases, they never hear back and, if they do, the quotes are often eye-wateringly expensive.

According to the latest state of trade survey by the Federation of Master Builders (FMB), 61pc of businesses report being affected by skilled labour shortages. This, in combination with rising materials prices and increased wage and National Insurance costs, means that 61pc of companies surveyed by the FMB have also increased the prices they charge for their work.

“Since Covid and Brexit, households have found themselves competing for cleaners, gardeners and tradesmen in a way they simply didn’t have to before,” says Robin Edwards, of London buying agency Curetons.

“It was a double whammy, as Covid accelerated people leaving the workforce and changed work preferences, while Brexit reduced the pool of migrant labour that had previously supplied many domestic service roles.”

The recruitment crisis means that, in many parts of the country, securing household staff can feel like a competitive sport, with homeowners increasingly willing to fight dirty.

“In the Cotswolds, we have seen people offering cleaners cash on the spot to jump ship from a neighbour, while village politics have also erupted into full-blown feuds over the loyalty of a particularly talented gardener,” says Harry Gladwin, of buying agency The Buying Solution.

“Increasingly, success in the countryside is measured not just by the splendour of your house but by whether you have managed to hold on to the cleaner who knows how to coax red wine out of a Cotswold stone floor.”

A homeowner from Essex says the cleaning company she used would often cancel just before the cleaners were due. “This was usually because the manager had received a call from the council for an emergency clean or even from the police for specialist forensic cleaning, which was presumably better paid,” she says. “I clean after the cleaners have been, but feel I have to keep them on as it’s better than nothing.”

According to official data from the Office for National Statistics, the number of people employed as “cleaners and domestics” has actually risen over the past year or so. After dropping sharply from 491,800 in 2021 to 460,300 in 2022, it recovered last year to 492,200.

Crucially, however, the survey only picks up people who work for employers registered for PAYE and VAT. As such, many will probably be cleaning commercial premises such as schools and hospitals, and the numbers will not reflect the thousands of cleaners who work mainly for private households being paid in cash.

And this is where the worst shortages are to be found. The British Cleaning Council (BCC) says recruiting cleaners is particularly difficult in London, where 60pc of the industry workforce was born overseas. Other regions have historically relied much less heavily on migrant labour – in north-east England, for instance, only 4pc of those working in cleaning were born overseas, the BCC says.

“We met a new client recently who has just lost their trusted Bulgarian cleaner of many years as she decided to move back home – and we’re hearing this more and more,” says Simmi Sangha, of property company Eccord, which manages 150 homes across London.

“Those who are staying are increasing their hourly rates and most clients are paying without hesitation because they don’t want to risk losing them.”

While the most recent ONS figures, dating from April 2024, showed that the median pay for those employed as full-time cleaners was £12.12 an hour, anecdotal evidence suggests households are paying much more than this – in London and the South East £25 an hour is common. With some cleaners in the capital now reportedly running waiting lists for clients, homeowners are going to increasingly desperate measures to attract and keep them.

“Lavish birthday and Christmas presents and bonuses are also common,” Edwards says. “I know of one client who lent their housekeeper the free use of their villa in Portugal for a week and another, an aesthetic doctor, who gives his cleaner free Botox.”

There’s also a recruitment crisis in horticulture, with the number of gardeners and landscape gardeners falling by 29,000 between 2019 and 2021, according to the ONS’s Labour Force Survey.

The sector relied heavily on seasonal workers from the EU before Brexit, while people in their 50s and 60s are retiring and are not being replaced by new apprentices. Fewer young people are willing to embark on a career that’s often hard work but poorly paid, despite the extensive knowledge needed for skilled roles.

Qualified self-employed gardeners will charge an average rate of between £30 and £41 an hour, according to research by the Gardeners Guild, although this assumes you book them for a full day and are a regular customer. Irregular customers may expect to pay more.

In some cases, they face paying significantly more. A client of Eccord in Chelsea, south-west London, was recently quoted £600 plus VAT per month for a gardener to come in once a fortnight to do a basic tidy up of a small terraced rear garden and a few window boxes.

Meanwhile, Jennie Hancock, of the West Sussex buying agency Property Acquisitions, has a client who bought a house and paid a “significant premium” to persuade the gardener to stay on. “This wasn’t only because he was excellent and knew the garden inside out, but he’d also worked on the Capability Brown gardens at nearby Petworth Park, which brought a certain cachet,” she says.

And they’re the lucky ones. It took Katie Neale, from the buying agency Haringtons UK, five attempts before she could even get a gardener to reply to her requests to come and work at her own rural home. “When he finally came, after an hour of wandering around with his hands on his hips, he declared the garden ‘a life’s work’ and promised a quote that never materialised,” she says. “I now see him every week at my neighbour’s house, where he ducks behind the hedges whenever I drive past.”

And don’t get Emma Horton started on builders. She and her husband would like to knock a wall through from their kitchen to the dining room, add bifold windows in the kitchen and put a utility room into part of the garage. Yet, just as for cleaners, she’s been ghosted by two builders and told by a third that he can’t do the job for another year.

“We have another company coming round and I am hopeful that we might get somewhere, but the process is so frustrating and time-consuming,” she says. “I wonder whether it’s because it’s not a particularly huge job, but I wish they would just respond, even if it’s to turn us down.”

Jeremy Gray, of the FMB, says it has long been tricky to hire a builder as construction has never kept up with demand. “Good ones are booked up 12 to 18 months ahead and that has always been the case – if anyone said they could start next week, I’d run a mile,” he says. “Cost pressures have become more pronounced since Covid particularly, and builders are a lot better paid than people realise.”

And spare a thought for the wealthy, who are also battling for staff to look after their houses and gardens. The 2025 Estate Staff Salary Survey by Knight Frank estate agency, released in June, found that 87pc of estates and country houses said it was becoming harder to attract good staff, while almost 60pc said retaining workers was also becoming more difficult.

This comes despite years of steady wage increases. The average salary of an estate director has risen from £83,224 in 2020 to £103,221; a housekeeper’s pay has gone from £24,292 to £30,426; and a head gardener’s income has risen from £28,806 to £36,428, according to Knight Frank.

There are several factors to blame, including rural locations that are a barrier to recruitment, says Alastair Paul of Knight Frank’s rural consultancy.

In the countryside, the Covid-era race for space that suddenly ratcheted up the demand for rural living was also a factor, although that has now calmed down, points out Philip Eddell, the director of Eddell & Co property consultancy.

It’s not uncommon for roles for housekeepers, gardeners and maintenance teams to sit empty for months, says Kathryn Donkin, of Strutt & Parker. “I know an estate that has been advertising for a maintenance worker for nearly a year,” she adds.

data.title: Tube stations shut down as staff jet off on summer holidays
data.description:
London Underground stations closed for a combined total of more than 45 days over the summer holiday season because of staff shortages, it has been revealed.

Stops on the Tube network were forced to shut down 387 times during normal hours in the months of June, July and August in 2025 – three times as many times as in the spring.

Underground stations in the capital were closed for a total of 65,786 minutes during the period – the equivalent of 45 days, 16 hours and 26 minutes, according to Transport for London (TfL) data.

Station closure figures covering the last five years, obtained following a freedom of information request by The Telegraph, show large seasonal discrepancies for disruption caused by staff shortages.

The findings have prompted critics to call on TfL to “get a grip” and better monitor worker rotas during popular holiday months.

It comes as Sir Sadiq Khan, the Mayor of London, prepares to increase Tube fares by an inflation-busting 5.8 per cent.

‘Not good enough’

Susan Hall, leader of the London Assembly Conservatives, said it was “ludicrous” that station closures occurred more frequently during the summer.

“It’s about time TfL started considering the customers,” she said. “It’s very inconvenient when a station closes; people have got to get to work.

“It’s not good enough, especially when fares are going up.”

Between January 1 2020 and October 2025, there were nearly twice as many closures in summer (1,562) as there were in total during the spring months of March, April and May (802).

Chancery Lane station was the worst affected after being closed to force 64 times, while Lancaster Gate closed 54 times and Covent Garden on 39 occasions.

Regulations require some stations to have a minimum number of staff, meaning TfL is legally obliged to close a station if worker numbers are insufficient.

During shortages, workers are moved to well-connected stations with higher usage and step free access in a “prioritisation” process, The Telegraph understands.

As a result, stations that do not meet this criterion are closed “more frequently”.

Network ‘not properly staffed’

Elliot Keck, campaigns director at the TaxPayers’ Alliance, said: “These figures raise serious questions about how London’s transport network is being run.

“While staffing levels have increased, closures keep piling up. That strongly suggests the network still isn’t properly staffed to cope with sickness and holidays.

“Londoners should not be expected to tolerate routine closures because basic cover hasn’t been put in place. TfL needs to get a grip.”

Nick Dent, TfL’s director of customer operations, said: “We are sorry if customers have been affected by short-notice closures at some of our Tube stations.

“We are aware of the inconvenience that station closures can cause, which is why we only close stations as a last resort and always work to minimise the length of time a station is closed for.”

He added: “We do all we can to minimise station closures, and we have an established and flexible rostering system to ensure stations are sufficiently staffed throughout the year.

“We are also introducing a new staffing model that will help reduce station closures by enabling us to deploy staff more flexibly.”

data.title: Everything you need to know about remortgaging your home
data.description:
If your mortgage deal is coming to an end in the next few months, it is time to start the process of remortgaging to switch to a new one.

Getting started early means you’ll avoid ending up on your lender’s standard variable rate, SVR, which is usually much more expensive.

Here, Telegraph Money explains how the remortgaging process works, and how to get the best rate for your mortgage.

  • What is remortgaging?
  • Remortgaging process
  • Where can I find the cheapest interest rates?
  • How long should I lock in a rate for?
  • How can I get the best rate for my mortgage?

What is remortgaging?

Remortgaging refers to taking out a new mortgage deal whenever the fixed rate expires – so, for example, finding a new two-year fix when your current two-year fix runs out.

Technically, you only remortgage if you’re moving to a different lender, but the term can also be used to describe those switching to a different deal with the same lender – but this is actually a “product transfer”.

Why should you remortgage?

There are lots of reasons why you should remortgage, including:

  • Finding a competitive deal: The cheapest rates tend to be on offer from fixed-rate or tracker mortgages, so it’s a good idea to switch straight from one deal to another once your term ends. Not only will your monthly repayments be lower, but you’ll also pay less interest over the course of the mortgage term. You may also save money if your home’s value has increased; the extra equity will mean you could be borrowing a smaller proportion of the total property cost – and a 60pc loan tends to be cheaper than a 75pc loan, for example.
  • Avoiding your lender’s standard variable rate (SVR): A lender’s SVR is the most expensive option, and can also fluctuate at the lender’s discretion. In general, these rates will go up and down in accordance with the Bank Rate, but they’re not officially linked.
  • Protecting yourself against fluctuations: Opting for a fixed-rate deal means you’ll have the peace of mind of knowing what your repayments will be for the duration of the term. This can be particularly beneficial if mortgage rates rise during the term.
  • Borrowing more: If you want to remove equity from your home, you’ll need to go through the remortgaging process so a lender can decide whether or not to lend you more.

However, there are some instances where it could be worth allowing your mortgage to revert to your lender’s SVR – for example, if you’re planning to move house in the near future and cannot port a new mortgage deal to another property, or if you’re due to finish repaying your mortgage very soon.

In these sorts of cases, the fees you’ll pay for taking out a new deal and ending it early may not be worth it.

Remortgaging process

1. Start looking around – six months in advance

The remortgaging process can take a while, so if you want to avoid spending time on your lender’s SVR, it’s a good idea to get the process started a few months before your current deal is scheduled to end.

It’s worth making a note of when this will be, but your lender should also get in touch with you well in advance of your deal ending.

You might want to contact a mortgage broker to start looking for deals in advance – some lenders will let you hold a mortgage in principle for up to six months before the deal starts, though some have cut this back to three or four months.

2. Ask for a closing balance

Also known as a “redemption statement”, your lender will issue a statement that shows how much is on your remaining mortgage loan, which you’ll need to borrow if you remortgage.

This information will be required when you make an application to a new lender.

3. Decide on a new mortgage

A mortgage broker can help you make this decision, and you may find our guide on whether to choose a fixed-rate or tracker mortgage helpful, along with our mortgage best buys, which show the top rates on offer.

Alternatively, you may want to apply for an interest-only mortgage.

Your broker, if you’re using one, can start the application process once you’ve found a deal you want.

4. Instruct a solicitor

This is a necessary step if you’re moving to a different lender. A solicitor or conveyancer will sort the paperwork required for the new contract, such as drawing up the mortgage deeds.

5. Go through affordability checks

Your new lender will need to check that you can afford the mortgage you’re applying for. If you’re simply moving from one repayment mortgage to another, the process should be relatively straightforward, as you’ll probably need to borrow less than you did at the start of your previous deal.

However, if you’re moving to an interest-only deal, or want to remortgage to take equity out of your property, then the affordability checks may be more rigorous.

You’ll likely need some of the following documents:

  • Recent payslips
  • Utility bills
  • Bank statements
  • ID (usually a passport or driving licence)
  • Proof of address.

6. Mortgage valuation

Lenders issue valuations to check that the property is worth the amount you want to borrow for it. This is not always done in person – it may be done online, or someone may drive past the property. In some cases, lenders will charge for a valuation to be carried out.

7. Get the mortgage offer

Assuming the previous steps all go to plan, your solicitor should receive a mortgage offer letter. It may contain certain conditions that you’ll have to fulfil when the deal begins (such as paying off remaining credit card debt).

If your circumstances change between receiving the offer and moving to the new mortgage, you should tell your new lender.

8. Move to the new deal

When the time comes, your solicitor will be responsible for requesting the money from your new lender and paying off the debt owing to the old one.

They should also update the Land Registry and, in some cases, the title deeds with the new details.

If you’re switching to a new deal with the same lender, some of these steps may be skipped – such as instructing a solicitor.

Where are mortgage rates now?

At the time of writing, the average two-year fixed rate is 4.84pc; the average five-year fix is priced slightly more expensive at 4.91pc, according to analyst Moneyfacts.

The average two-year tracker is 4.66pc, while the average SVR is 7.27pc as of Dec 1.

It’s possible to get much cheaper deals than the average rates, but the cheapest are often reserved for those who are remortgaging and seeking to borrow no more than 60pc of the property’s value.

One decision lots of homeowners have been struggling with is whether to commit to a fixed-rate deal or opt for a tracker mortgage that’s linked to the Bank Rate. Should interest rates reduce further, those with tracker mortgages will see their mortgage bills get cheaper – until then, you might find yourself on a deal that’s more expensive than the cheapest fixed deals.

Our guide on the fixed rate vs tracker dilemma can help.

What is happening to mortgage rates?

Mortgage rates have broadly been on the way down this year, mirroring cuts to the Bank Rate.

Many factors play into the Bank of England’s decision whether to hold, cut or increase the Bank Rate, and there are other market forces that affect how much lenders charge for borrowing, such as inflation.

The consumer prices index (CPI) measure of inflation has remained significantly above the Bank’s target of 2pc, which has meant slower cuts to the Bank Rate in case cheaper borrowing causes prices to surge.

Rachel Springall, of Moneyfacts.co.uk, said: “Mortgage rates continue on the downward trend, and November was particularly fruitful for fixed rate cuts. The re-pricing by lenders led to the average five-year fixed rate dropping below 5pc for the first time in over two years and sits at its lowest point since before the ‘mini-Budget’ in September 2022, alongside its two-year counterpart.

“However, those who locked into a cheap fixed deal five years ago will need to accept that they will have to cover higher repayments. The Bank of England expects 3.9 million households to refinance on to higher rates over the next three years.”

Where can I find the cheapest interest rates?

A mortgage broker can help find the best mortgage deals for you, but it’s also possible to do your own research.

Our guide to the best mortgage rates can help get you started. Its tables are updated every day between Tuesday and Saturday, featuring the top deals for purchase rates and remortgaging, across two, five and 10-year fixed rates, as well as the best variable-rate options.

It is important to remember that the lowest interest rates do not necessarily equate to the best deal. High fees can sometimes outweigh marginal savings on similarly priced interest rates.

Lenders commonly set fees between £899 and £1,999, but some use percentages instead, such as 0.5pc of the overall loan amount.

Adrian Anderson, of broker Anderson Harris, said: “Don’t just look at the headline rate. Consider the overall cost of the deal, including any fees and whether the lender will pay for a mortgage valuation and legal conveyance, which most banks do.”

If you are locking in for longer than two years then be sure to check any repayment penalties which would apply should your circumstances change and you need to exit the deal early – these can run into the thousands of pounds.

Our mortgage increase calculator can show how much your bills will change as a result of remortgaging to a new deal.

How long should I lock in a rate for?

Thousands of today’s homeowners with fixed mortgages are still on rates agreed when the Bank Rate was far lower than it is today. This means they are likely to face a steep increase in their payments when they reach the end of their current deal and take out a new loan.

This is because while fixed rates may be falling, the cost of borrowing is still inflated when compared with mortgages which were locked in five years ago. Borrowers coming off those deals and searching for a new rate are very likely to face higher rates.

Brokers have been suggesting a typical borrower should fix for two years as this would minimise the amount of time spent fixed on an inflated rate.

Nicholas Mendes, of broker John Charcol, said: “For borrowers evaluating their options, the choice between fixed and variable rates remains key. A fixed-rate mortgage provides stability, locking in payments for a set period, whereas a variable-rate mortgage tracks the Bank of England’s rate, offering potential savings if rates decline further.

“For those comfortable with a slightly higher initial rate, a shorter-term fixed deal or a tracker mortgage offers flexibility, allowing a switch should rates fall further. Conversely, a longer-term fix provides certainty and protection against potential market volatility.

“Whether to wait or lock in a deal now depends on personal financial circumstances, risk tolerance, and market conditions. Seeking professional mortgage advice is crucial to making an informed decision.”

How can I get the best rate for my mortgage?

Increase your equity

Borrowers who put more equity into a property make themselves less-risky customers, increasing the chances a lender will be happier to offer them lower interest rates. Banks use the term “loan-to-value ratio” (LTV) to label how much they lend a borrower against a home.

If you have a repayment mortgage, you’ll naturally increase your equity over time, but if you want to speed up the process then you can consider overpaying your mortgage.

For example, a £160,000 mortgage on a £200,000 home would be a loan-to-value of 80pc. A lower loan-to-value and bigger deposit will usually unlock lower interest rates. Mr Anderson said: “It can make quite a big difference to the amount of interest you pay over the term of the deal. So if you have cash available you may want to consider paying down part of the mortgage to access a better rate.”

Our mortgage overpayment calculator can help you weigh up whether you’re better off overpaying your mortgage, or putting your extra cash into a savings account.

Consider an offset mortgage

Households with a significant cash pile could also access lower rates by using an offset mortgage. A handful of banks allow borrowers to reduce the cost of their loan using cash held in an account with the same lender. For example, a customer borrowing a £500,000 mortgage and with £200,000 in savings would only pay interest on £300,000 of the loan, but would forfeit any interest on the cash pot.

Based on a mortgage interest rate of 4.5pc, this would reduce the monthly interest from £1,873 to £1,124 – a saving of £749.

Mr Anderson said: “Offset mortgages are proving especially popular as tax thresholds are shrinking and reducing households’ personal allowances.

“There should be no tax to pay on savings used to offset the mortgage balance and you should not be paying interest on the mortgage balance offset by the cash funds. The account should also be instant access so you still have access to liquidity if circumstances change.”

Borrowers opting for a more specialist mortgage, such as an offset deal, should consult a mortgage adviser.

What about interest-only mortgages?

Any homeowners struggling to pay their mortgage bills are able to switch to interest-only deals without a formal repayment plan. City watchdog the Financial Conduct Authority announced the change in 2023 in an attempt to help lenders provide mortgage forbearance at scale.

However, once the temporary interest-free period is over, homeowners must make up their repayments. To switch to a permanent interest-only deal, you’ll still need a credible repayment plan.

data.title: Amazon gets green light to deliver by drone
data.description:
Amazon has been given the green light to launch Britain’s first drone delivery service, which will see parcels dropped into customers’ gardens.

The Civil Aviation Authority (CAA) has approved changes to airspace rules in the area around Darlington, County Durham, where the company plans to offer the service.

This means Amazon could launch the service imminently, although the company did not specify when it plans its maiden flights.

It expects to operate up to 10 flights per hour from a local warehouse, 12 hours a day, seven days a week.

Amazon has faced opposition from some local residents over potential aircraft noise, as well as from model aircraft flyers, who warn it could interfere with their hobby.

The company has said its drones are designed to minimise noise, noting that the disruption will be less than that from delivery drivers slamming doors and reversing.

“This is an exciting step towards bringing drone delivery to customers in Darlington. We’re continuing to work closely with Darlington council and the Civil Aviation Authority on this innovative first for the UK,” Amazon said.

The company applied to the CAA in March last year and hoped to begin deliveries before Christmas, but the regulator did not confirm approval until Friday.

Autonomous drone flights in the UK require operators to reserve airspace for their aircraft, as rules governing how they detect and avoid other airborne vehicles have not yet been finalised.

Amazon says the drone flights will allow it to deliver packages in under two hours. The deliveries have been a dream of Jeff Bezos, Amazon’s founder, for more than a decade.

The flights will take off from a helipad at Amazon’s local distribution centre, with the aircraft flying at between 55 and 85 metres in the air.

Amazon first tested drone flights in Cambridgeshire a decade ago but ended testing, in part due to frustration with airspace restrictions.

It has since been carrying out drone delivery tests in various American cities. The US Federal Aviation Authority recently said it was investigating after one of the company’s drones severed an internet cable, cutting off a customer’s service.

Another company, Apian, has been using drones to transport blood samples between London hospitals and plans to expand the service.

The CAA was contacted for comment. The regulator has said it hopes that laws will allow routine drone flights by next year.

Amazon’s permission for drone flights is temporary, lasting until June, although the company could apply for a 12-month extension.

data.title: Elon Musk’s X faces action on UK ban within days
data.description:
Labour has threatened Elon Musk’s X with action over a potential ban within days amid backlash over its artificial intelligence undressing women and children in photographs.

Liz Kendall, the Technology Secretary, said the media regulator Ofcom would take action against X “in days, not weeks” and use “the full legal powers Parliament has given them”.

Sir Keir Starmer said on Thursday that he had asked Ofcom for “all options to be on the table” – including potentially blocking access to X in Britain, where it has around 20 million users.

The move to ramp up pressure comes after Mr Musk sought to dampen the controversy surrounding his company’s Grok AI bot by limiting access to paying users only.

Ms Kendall said: “Sexually manipulating images of women and children is despicable and abhorrent. It is an insult and totally unacceptable for Grok to still allow this if you’re willing to pay for it. I expect Ofcom to use the full legal powers Parliament has given them.

“I – and more importantly, the public - would expect to see Ofcom update on next steps in days, not weeks.”

She added that if platforms failed to comply with Ofcom’s guidance to protect women and girls, the Government was prepared to take further action.

This week, a UK internet watchdog warned it had uncovered images created with X’s AI chatbot, Grok, that had been shared on a dark web forum that would constitute illegal child sexual abuse material.

X, formerly known as Twitter, has been flooded by users asking Grok to remove clothes from pictures of real women including politicians, celebrities and the Princess of Wales.

Under the Online Safety Act, Ofcom can apply for a court order to bar access to sites that repeatedly refuse to block illegal content. This includes non-consensual intimate images and child sexual abuse material.

The powers would compel internet providers like BT and mobile app stores to remove the offending service.

The mechanism has never been tested by the watchdog and would represent an extraordinary expansion of a free speech rift with the US.

Mr Musk appeared to suggest on Friday that a ban on X would amount to censorship. 

Pointing to claims other AI programmes created non-sexualised images of women in bikinis, he posted on X: “They want any excuse for censorship.”

Last night, a US Representative in Donald Trump’s home state, Florida, threatened to introduce a bill imposing sanctions on the Prime Minister and the UK in response to the threat.

For over a week, tens of thousands of X users have posted pictures of real women while tagging Grok with requests such as “put her in a bikini” and other more lewd demands.

The images have prompted fury from Labour politicians as well as US Democrats and some senior Republicans.

Three US Democratic senators wrote on Friday to Tim Cook, the chief executive of Apple, and Google’s Sundar Pichai, calling on them to block X and Grok from their app stores.

They wrote: “Turning a blind eye to X’s egregious behaviour would make a mockery of your moderation practices.”

On Friday, X – which is part of Mr Musk’s company xAI – appeared to limit the number of users who can generate AI images of women using its app to paying subscribers, blocking large numbers of requests for sexualised images.

However, the Prime Minister’s spokesman said the change was “insulting” and effectively turned the “creation of unlawful images into a premium service”.

Ms Kendall said the UK would be banning so-called nudification apps similar to Grok under the upcoming crime and policing bill. She added that Labour was preparing further powers that would criminalise the creation of intimate AI images in the coming weeks.

Ms Kendall said: “I would remind xAI that the Online Safety Act includes the power to block services from being accessed in the UK if they refuse to comply with UK law. If Ofcom decide to use those powers, they will have our full support.”

An Ofcom spokesman said: “We urgently made contact on Monday and set a firm deadline of today to explain themselves, to which we have received a response. We’re now undertaking an expedited assessment as a matter of urgency and will provide further updates shortly.”

An X spokesman pointed The Telegraph to a post by the tech giant from Sunday: “We take action against illegal content on X, including child sexual abuse material, by removing it, permanently suspending accounts and working with local governments and law enforcement as necessary.”

data.title: Sainsbury’s blames Reeves’s Budget for Argos slump
data.description:
Sainsbury’s has blamed the Budget for driving a drop in sales at Argos as Rachel Reeves’s tax raid hammered consumer confidence in the run-up to Christmas.

The supermarket said fewer people bought expensive items such as furniture during the festive period, with customers having “pulled back” on spending in November.

Simon Roberts, the chief executive of Sainsbury’s, said: “Obviously there was some caution, particularly as we came towards the Budget.”

He said demand at Argos over Christmas had then been “subdued”, which led to the brand posting a 2.2pc decline in sales in the six weeks to Jan 3.

Mr Roberts said: “More broadly, customers continue to look very closely at what they’re spending.”

The Chancellor rattled shoppers before Christmas by fuelling speculation over tax increases. She said in early November that everyone would “have to contribute” to help put Britain in a stronger position.

The comments were seen as a strong indication that Ms Reeves was preparing to increase income tax – a plan she later scrapped following a backlash in Westminster.

However, bosses said this had already filtered into weaker spending before Christmas.

Ms Reeves’s decision to freeze income tax thresholds in the Budget is also expected to have hurt people’s appetite to spend on non-essential items.

Mr Roberts said: “We’ve got to make sure we present the best value in that context as we progress through this year.”

The warning over pressure on Argos prompted a sell-off in Sainsbury’s shares, making it the biggest faller in the FTSE 100 on Friday. Shares in Sainsbury’s were down 5.3pc, wiping around £400m from its market capitalisation.

Mr Roberts said the weaker performance at Argos was also driven by more competition from Chinese sellers Temu and Shein. That competition hit visitor numbers to the Argos website, he said.

Mr Roberts added that an upcoming government crackdown on a tax loophole used by the Chinese online giants – unveiled at the most recent Budget – should happen “as soon as possible”.

The Chancellor has said the plan is to change the de minimis rule, whereby products worth less than £135 are exempt from UK import duties, by 2029.

Ms Reeves has said this is part of a drive to “stand up for the British high street” as she seeks to prevent overseas retailers from undercutting UK companies because of the loopholes.

Mr Roberts said: “I’m sure that they’ll be working hard to do that because a level playing field in this market is really important.”

The growing pressure at Argos will prompt fresh speculation that Sainsbury’s will be seeking to offload the brand, which has proven to be a drag on performance in recent years.

In the Christmas period, Sainsbury’s said it had won more customers in its grocery business with sales up 5.1pc in the six weeks to Jan 3, 2026.

Sainsbury’s has recently been pursuing a “food first” strategy, where it has allocated more space in stores to fresh produce.

The Telegraph revealed last summer that Sainsbury’s was in talks with JD.com about a potential deal for Argos, although the proposed sale later collapsed.

However, Mr Roberts has since fuelled speculation that Sainsbury’s could still sell the business, saying recently that the process with JD.com helped it to realise that “Argos is separable”.

Speaking to analysts in November, Mr Roberts said: “It’s not something that’s wholly straightforward immediately, but it’s something that we identified a route through.”
