data.title: Why banning X would be harder than Labour imagines
data.description:
How do you solve a problem like X? For Britain, the answer might appear seemingly simple: ban the site altogether.

Sir Keir Starmer has launched a full-scale assault as Westminster seeks to rein in Grok, the chatbot owned by Elon Musk’s social network – formerly known as Twitter.

On Friday, ministers said they would “fully support” any move by UK regulators to ban access to X and the Grok chatbot. 

The offensive, in response to Grok being used to undress pictures of women and children, suggest the website could be wiped off the face of the map if Musk fails to act over the deepfake scandal.

But a ban would be an extraordinary step – and harder to enact than first appears.

While the number of Brits using X has fallen under Musk’s ownership, seven million adults still use the site every day, according to figures from Ofcom.

And it would generate a backlash from users and almost certainly lead to retaliation from the White House, where Musk has close ties thanks to his friendship with Donald Trump.

It would also see Britain join a ignominious club, whose other members include China, Russia, Iran and North Korea.

Grok, X and Musk are in trouble because the chatbot has been creating tens of thousands of realistic images of women, and in some cases children, in minimal clothing or in sexual poses.

These deepfake images are typically altered versions of real people, with women including the Princess of Wales re-drawn at the request of users.

In response, Sir Keir and female celebrities such as Maya Jama have urged X to disable the feature – but seemingly to no avail.

While the company has removed the function for the public, it has bundled the feature into its subscription package – to users who fork out $30 a month.

The last resort

If Britain follows up with the nuclear option and outlaws X, it would fall to industry watchdog Ofcom to wield the knife.

The watchdog was given new online safety powers last year allowing it to block websites in the UK.

In recent months, the regulator has already raised the prospect of blocking other websites that fall foul of the laws.

In response to the deepfake scandal, Ofcom has threatened X with an online safety investigation, saying it had made urgent contact with the company and that it was aware of “serious concerns” that the bot was generating sexualised images of children.

“We will undertake a swift assessment to determine whether there are potential compliance issues that warrant investigation,” the regulator said.

Under the Online Safety Act, Ofcom has powers to force websites and apps to shield children from adult and harmful material, and all users from illegal content.

Sites available in Britain must check users’ ages to stop children seeing pornography and prevent illegal content such as child abuse images.

Powers can range from censure and fines to “business disruption measures”, such as requiring payment providers and advertisers to cut off services.

Ultimately, these could also apply to internet service providers such as BT, ordering them not to connect users to the site.

This is seen as a last resort, but it is not out of the question.

In a separate case, Ofcom said this week that it was preparing to seek a court order that could block an unnamed suicide forum in the UK, after it refused to provide the regulator with a risk assessment that websites are required to submit under online safety laws.

‘Insulting to victims’

A similar outcome may follow for 4chan, the controversial anonymous web forum that has also refused to comply with the laws.

Lawyers for the two websites say that they are based in the US and have no British operations; 4chan has sued Ofcom in American courts.

Blocking X would be a different matter altogether.

The company has made efforts to comply with the law, handing Ofcom a risk assessment and introducing age controls that stop children seeing explicit material.

Lorna Woods, a professor of internet law at the University of Essex, says X could face punishment from Ofcom if it had failed to inform the regulator about changes to Grok that have allowed the bot to generate the offending images.

The company could face investigation for allowing the sharing of illegal images.

This week, the Internet Watch Foundation, Britain’s child abuse hotline, said it had seen illegal images, apparently created using Grok and posted on a separate dark web forum.

While creating this is illegal in Britain, it is unclear if X, rather than the user, would be liable, and crucially, the images do not appear to have been published on X itself. Grok allows users to create images privately.

Woods says other deepfake images that were posted on X could constitute illegal content, if they are considered intimate images shared against the subject’s will – a threshold for criminal content under the online safety laws.

However, the mere existence of them would not be enough for punishment. Ofcom would have to show that X had failed to comply with the regulator’s codes of conduct on how to deal with them.

“The codes don’t deal with stopping the problem happening,” says Woods. “The codes are dealing with clearing up afterwards. But that’s all Ofcom can look for the company to do at the moment.”

X, which says it removes child abuse material and suspends accounts that ask it to be created, could argue that it is taking action.

“If they did a 4chan and say ‘we’re not going to play ball, go away’, then I think that increases the likelihood that Ofcom has to do something more dramatic. But X is much more of a business,” adds Woods.

X’s move to limit Grok’s public image posts to premium users was criticised by Downing Street as “insulting to victims”.

Potential ‘fierce’ backlash

While Mr Musk has publicly criticised overseas governments that seek to rein in his company, and has called the Online Safety Act “suppression of the people”, X’s actions have been less combative.

It has complied with Brazilian requests to appoint local representatives to avoid a ban and obeyed Indian takedown orders.

Regulators would also find it impossible to ignore the wider implications of blocking X.

“The backlash to a ban would be fierce, and Starmer would be accused of suppressing free speech,” says Ed Newton-Rex of Fairly Trained, a non-profit that seeks to force AI companies to comply with copyright laws.

He adds that he thinks banning it would be the right move if X doesn’t change course.

Britain would also face potential diplomatic retaliation.

On Christmas Eve, the US State Department barred five Europeans, including Thierry Breton, the architect of the EU’s social media regulations, from entering the US.

Anna Paulina Luna, a Donald Trump ally in the US Congress, said on Friday that Sir Keir should be sanctioned if X is banned in Britain.

However, Technology Secretary Liz Kendall later ramped up the threats. 

“Sexually manipulating images of women and children is despicable and abhorrent. It is an insult and totally unacceptable for Grok to still allow this if you’re willing to pay for it. I expect Ofcom to use the full legal powers Parliament has given them,” she said. 

As long as ministers talk tough, the threat may continue. But given what is at stake, it would be a brave move.

data.title: NHS poised to test new ‘longevity’ drug to boost Britain’s lifespans
data.description:
The NHS is preparing to test an experimental anti-ageing drug that could boost lifespans and treat debilitating diseases.

The British pharmaceutical start-up LinkGevity has developed what it claims is “the Swiss army knife of drugs” and has begun talks with officials to initiate clinical trials.

The drug is designed to prevent a type of cell death, necrosis, which contributes to a range of age-related diseases. The NHS body Health Innovation East, which helps drug developers navigate Britain’s health bureaucracy to commercialise scientific breakthroughs, has agreed to expedite the path to clinical approval.

If the success that LinkGevity has already reported in animal studies is replicated in human kidney patients, the drug could become the world’s first proven anti-ageing treatment.

Carina Kern, one of the two sisters behind LinkGevity, said: “The discussions we’re going to be having with them will be around ensuring we pick the best hospital sites for our clinical trial and getting the drug approved as rapidly as possible so you can have the maximum benefit for patients.”

She said Health Innovation East’s “whole motto is how do you get something to the patient, and how do you accelerate that timeline”.

LinkGevity said this meant approval would likely take a few years rather than decades.

In addition to kidney disease, the start-up hopes its technology can address liver and other organ degeneration. LinkGevity has received funding from Nasa and the UK Space Agency to develop a treatment for the accelerated ageing that astronauts experience.

Ms Kern said the treatment could prove to be “the Swiss army knife of drugs” to help stop degeneration in multiple organs, adding: “These diseases rob people of their human dignity. There is a downward spiral of health decline that you get, and the ambition is very much to try to alleviate that for the first time.

“Of course, the natural outcome of that is if you’re taking out issues that are robbing you of your health and limiting the length of your life, of course, you will live longer and healthier.”

Life expectancy in the UK

It follows years in which scientists have been racing to find a “cure” for ageing. Tech moguls, including Jeff Bezos, Peter Thiel and Sam Altman, have poured millions into longevity companies working to crack the code for longer life.

For generations, medical breakthroughs delivered increasing life expectancies. However, life expectancy is now stalling across Europe. Figures published in The Lancet Public Health found that, on average, life expectancy increased by 0.23 years per year between 1990 and 2011, then declined by 0.15 years per year between 2011 and 2019.

In England, life expectancy has risen more slowly than in the rest of Europe, up by just 0.07 years on average between 2011 and 2019.

Meanwhile, a separate analysis found that the UK had the lowest life expectancy in Western Europe. In 2024, the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development found that people born in Great Britain and Northern Ireland could expect to live to about 80.9 years.

This compares with an average of 81.5 years across the 27 European Union countries. Experts have pointed to obesity, bad diet and a lack of physical activity as the key driving factors behind the gap with the rest of the EU.

Under Labour’s 10-year health plan, the Government says it wants to “halve the gap in healthy life expectancy between the richest and poorest regions, while increasing it for everyone, and to raise the healthiest generation of children ever”.

data.title: NHS poised to test new ‘longevity’ drug to boost Britain’s lifespans
data.description:
The NHS is preparing to test an experimental anti-ageing drug that could boost lifespans and treat debilitating diseases.

The British pharmaceutical start-up LinkGevity has developed what it claims is “the Swiss army knife of drugs” and has begun talks with officials to initiate clinical trials.

The drug is designed to prevent a type of cell death, necrosis, which contributes to a range of age-related diseases. The NHS body Health Innovation East, which helps drug developers navigate Britain’s health bureaucracy to commercialise scientific breakthroughs, has agreed to expedite the path to clinical approval.

If the success that LinkGevity has already reported in animal studies is replicated in human kidney patients, the drug could become the world’s first proven anti-ageing treatment.

Carina Kern, one of the two sisters behind LinkGevity, said: “The discussions we’re going to be having with them will be around ensuring we pick the best hospital sites for our clinical trial and getting the drug approved as rapidly as possible so you can have the maximum benefit for patients.”

She said Health Innovation East’s “whole motto is how do you get something to the patient, and how do you accelerate that timeline”.

LinkGevity said this meant approval would likely take a few years rather than decades.

In addition to kidney disease, the start-up hopes its technology can address liver and other organ degeneration. LinkGevity has received funding from Nasa and the UK Space Agency to develop a treatment for the accelerated ageing that astronauts experience.

Ms Kern said the treatment could prove to be “the Swiss army knife of drugs” to help stop degeneration in multiple organs, adding: “These diseases rob people of their human dignity. There is a downward spiral of health decline that you get, and the ambition is very much to try to alleviate that for the first time.

“Of course, the natural outcome of that is if you’re taking out issues that are robbing you of your health and limiting the length of your life, of course, you will live longer and healthier.”

data.title: ​Oliver Marriott, author of The Property Boom who restored the Yorkshire manor house Grimstone Garth
data.description:
​Oliver Marriott​, who has died aged 86, was a financial journalist and property company director who restored an exceptional East Yorkshire mansion.

In his early career, Marriott was property editor of Investors Chronicle, financial editor of The Times and the author of well-praised investigative books, The Property Boom (1967) and Anatomy of a Merger (1970).

The former shone light on the machinations of new-rich tycoons in a fast-moving market, ruffling feathers and attracting several legal threats. The latter (co-authored with Robert Jones) explored the amalgamation of the ambitious General Electric Co led by Arnold Weinstock with the more traditional Associated Electrical Industries and English Electric to form a British industrial titan of its era.

In 1969, Marriott left journalism to become a property player in his own right. He joined forces with Jeffrey (later Lord) Sterling and Bruce McPhail in Sterling Guarantee Trust, acquired from the Slater Walker investment group, which they rapidly expanded by acquiring lame-duck companies with attractive assets – Marriott bringing depth of knowledge as the in-house m​arket analyst. 

The 1970 redevelopment of the Gamages department store site in Oxford Street was an early success.

The shrewd Sterling operation survived the crash of 1974 to emerge, with Bank of England and lenders’ support, as the rescuer of the much larger Town & City group – the sector’s biggest potential casualty as owner of Arndale shopping centres and other commercial properties valued at their peak at £600 million. Later, Sterling Guarantee merged with the shipping and property giant P&O, where Marriott was a director until 1991.

Meanwhile, in 1972 Marriott had bought Grimston Garth, a late-18th-century Gothic manor house designed by John Carr of York on a triangular footprint around a central hexagon, with castellated corner towers, in the Holderness district of East Yorkshire. Private, modest and independent in spirit, Marriott delighted in the restoration and care of the Grimston estate for the rest of his life.

Oliver Digby James Marriott was born in West Sussex on August 27 1939, one of four children of Rowland Marriott and his wife Evelyn, née Caillard.

Descended from a line of learned Leicestershire clergymen (though there were also ancestral connections in Yorkshire), Oliver was educated at Eton but shunned the family tradition of going on to study at Oxford. Instead, he became a clerk in an aggregates company in Pimlico and later with Rothschilds in the City.

In 1961 he was victorious as a Labour candidate for Lutterworth Rural District Council in Leicestershire, becoming the youngest councillor in the country. But he was bored with his banking job – and in the same year he discovered his true métier when he joined Investors Chronicle under the wing of its pioneering property editors Charles Gordon and Gary Arnott. 

After a stint on The Statist magazine, he moved in 1965 to The Sunday Times (where the then deputy editor William Rees-Mogg gave him a month off to finish the property book) and was financial editor of The Times from 1967 to 1969.

In his later career, Marriott was founder chairman of two smaller property companies, Churchbury Estates and Ilex, and a non-executive director of Shaftesbury, which owned tracts of Soho and Covent Garden.

A committed Eurosceptic, he stood in the 2005 general election as the Ukip candidate for the Conservative-held Beverley and Holderness constituency, attracting just 4.7 per cent of the poll with 2,336 votes.

His older brother Richard, a City financier who was Lord-Lieutenant of East Yorkshire, restored another historic house, Boynton Hall, a few miles north of Grimston; the brothers shared a particular love of books.

Passionate in his devotion to family life, Oliver Marriott married, in 1964, Lif Kristina Rolandsdottir from Stockholm, who survives him with their two daughters.

​Oliver Marriott, born August 27 1939, died December 5 2025

data.title: Electrical equipment shortage threatens net zero, Hitachi warns Miliband
data.description:
A shortage of electrical equipment threatens to derail Ed Miliband’s flagship net zero targets, a leading manufacturer has warned.

Hitachi, the world’s leading producer of high-voltage transformers, said a critical lack of kit could leave UK power operators unable to expand the grid fast enough to achieve clean electricity by 2030.

Laura Fleming, Hitachi’s UK managing director, said this shortfall was caused by unprecedented demand for electricity combined with a shortage of raw materials and skilled labour.

This has led to soaring prices and delayed deliveries for electrical equipment crucial to net zero.

Ms Fleming said: “In the UK and Europe, we’ve had 30-40 years of growing these [electrical] systems only on an as-needed basis and we’ve actually not seen electricity consumption go up dramatically.

“But we are now in a very different place where we’ve got very ageing systems with ageing equipment that needs replacing.

“At the same time, we’re trying to expand that grid because we’re moving from an energy system that is predominantly oil and gas-based to an energy system that is much more based on renewables, nuclear and a handful of other technologies.

“This is creating pressure on the system in a number of ways.”

The scale of the planned changes to Britain’s energy system is huge, inspired by the Energy Secretary’s move to embrace green electricity and slash the country’s reliance on fossil fuels.

Currently, the UK’s power stations, wind farms and other electricity producers have a total generating capacity of about 100 gigawatts.

This comes from a diverse mix of generators, ranging from massive gas-fired or nuclear power stations that produce steady, reliable power to intermittent wind and solar farms dependent on the weather.

Transformers are the unseen technology that unifies this system, converting the power they produce into the standardised voltages and frequencies needed by the UK’s power networks.

They work by “stepping up” power to the high voltages needed to efficiently transmit it long distances, typically around 400,000 volts.

Then, at the other end of the line, they reduce it back down to the 240 volts we need to run our homes.

But the 500 or so supergrid transformers that form the backbone of Britain’s high-voltage power transmission system are no longer enough to meet the nation’s needs.

Mostly installed between five and six decades ago, their age is showing – as highlighted by the catastrophic substation fire that shut down Heathrow last year, subsequently traced to a faulty 60-year-old transformer.

There are also far too few of them to support Mr Miliband’s ambitions of decarbonising the entire grid by 2030, along with a significant increase in power generation by 2050.

He has pledged to displace the oil and gas that now supply over 75pc of our total energy with green electricity from wind, solar and nuclear plants.

Such promises are easy to make but require mammoth upgrades to Britain’s grid.

Transformer capacity is measured in megavolt-amperes (MVA), with the UK currently boasting a capacity of about 170,000MVA.

By 2035, that will have to reach up to 280,000MVA – a two thirds increase – with even more needed by 2050.

That’s an unprecedented increase – and Britain’s problem is that its plans to expand and update its transmission network have coincided with everyone else’s across Europe.

The UK has three transmission operators: National Grid covering England and Wales, Scottish Power covering southern Scotland and SSE covering northern Scotland.

Recently, all three have found themselves pitched primarily against Tennet, the Dutch-German grid operator, for power transformers, subsea cables and other vital equipment.

For example, Britain wants to connect 21GW of offshore windfarms to its grid while Tennet has its own plans for 40GW of new wind.

The resulting power struggle has seen transformer order times surge from six months to four years, with prices rising 75pc in real terms since 2019.

Ms Fleming said it’s not just demand for raw materials that is driving up costs but the risk involved and the debt needed to finance new factories.

“Prices have gone up across the piece but it’s not just the costs for materials that have gone up,” she said. “It’s also the cost of finance. From a developer’s point of view, that probably adds more cost than the overall commodity costs.”

A 2025 report from the International Energy Agency set out the scale of the problem.

“Transformer lead times have almost doubled over the past two to three years,” it said. “Manufacturers have reported a record backlog of orders, increasing by one third in just one year.”

Artificial intelligence’s role

Hitachi is just one of several major players but its transformer backlog has now risen to $43bn (£32bn) – a trend being accelerated by the rise of AI.

Ms Fleming points to an internal Hitachi report – “Gridlocked? AI’s energy appetite” – which quantifies the impact of AI on the demand for transformers and associated equipment such as cables.

It calculates that Europe, including the UK, will need up to 200 giant new transformers by 2030 just for its new data centres, while America will install up to 460 and China another 130.

Add in the rest of the world and the total comes to 1,400 new transformers by 2030, just for AI alone.

Ms Fleming said this means that Britain faces a triple challenge.

It has to upgrade its old grid, expand it to meet the demands of decarbonisation and then expand it again for the needs of AI.

That’s a challenge but it could also be an opportunity – a chance for Britain to lead the world in manufacturing the parts needed for a future powered by green electricity. Will Britain grab that opportunity?

Serving the UK from Europe

Last month, regulator Ofgem announced its final approval for £90bn of investments proposed by National Grid, SSE and Scottish Power in pylons, substations and transformers, with the costs added to household and business energy bills.

So far, however, it’s clear that most of the kit needed to upgrade and expand the grid will be made abroad.

Ms Fleming said that most of the Hitachi transformers deployed in the UK will come from its factory near Łódź in Poland, assembled from components made in countries such as Sweden, Spain and Finland.

Meanwhile, the reluctance of investors like Hitachi to invest in the UK is largely related to its turbulent politics, especially around net zero.

For example, until the 2024 election, the target for decarbonising the grid was 2035 – a date chosen because of the long lead times needed for such massive infrastructure projects.

When Mr Miliband moved that date to 2030 after Labour came to power in July 2024, it prompted a radical political shift.

Notably, it destroyed the decades-long political consensus on climate in Westminster, a reversal that has seen the Conservatives row back from green policies that they introduced.

It also further opened the door to Reform, which has pledged to abolish net zero targets altogether.

In an interview last spring, Ms Fleming said that, faced with such turbulence, Hitachi could not take the risk of investing in UK factories.

“What we require beyond 2030 to invest in the UK is longer-term visibility,” she said. “So the visibility until 2030 is great but to be able to start considering longer-term investment, that visibility needs to go well beyond the political cycle.”

Such concerns surrounding the UK are already costing swathes of jobs and hundreds of millions of pounds in investment.

At Hitachi’s transformer factory in Sweden, for example, the company has doubled the workforce from 4,000 to 8,000 – about 1,000 new jobs a year, many to build transformers for the UK.

As Bruno Melles, Hitachi’s head of transformer manufacturing, puts it: “Right now, we believe that we can serve Great Britain from the investment that we made in Europe.

“The major factories supplying Britain are in Poland, Sweden, Germany, Spain and Italy. I have to say, all our factories are very, very active to ensure that we can supply Great Britain in a proper way – from Europe.”

A Government spokesman said: “Upgrading our electricity networks after years of underinvestment is essential to keep the lights on and ensure energy security for our country.

“We work closely with transmission operators who have robust long-term supply plans to secure essential components like transformers and with industry to make the most of Great British Energy’s £1bn plan to strengthen UK clean energy supply chains.”

data.title: Drug-driving causes more road deaths than alcohol
data.description:
Drugs are now a bigger factor in road deaths than alcohol, official figures show.

The number of deceased drivers who tested positive for drugs increased by 78 per cent – from 106 to 189 – in the decade to 2023, according to the Department for Transport (DfT) and police data.

By contrast, the number of dead motorists with alcohol proven to be present in their system rose by five per cent in the same period, from 162 to 171.

Sir Keir Starmer now stands accused of ignoring the bigger problem of drug-driving while planning to reduce the drink-driving limit. The Government’s proposals have prompted a backlash from MPs and publicans, who say they will put pubs under more pressure following an increase in business rates.

Britain lost an average of one pub each day in 2025, and industry bosses have warned that rising tax bills and wages, on top of higher energy costs, will drive hundreds more out of business.

The Telegraph has launched a campaign to save the UK’s pubs, calling on Labour to stop its assault on locals and cut tax and red tape.

Ministers are now expected to reverse their decision to impose higher costs on Britain’s struggling pubs, saying they are working on relief measures to be announced in the coming days. But the about-turn relates to jumps in business rates for landlords, not the new drink-drive limit.

DfT figures show that the percentage of fatal collisions in which drink-driving was involved has been relatively stable over the past 10 years, at 13 per cent.

However, the proportion in which drug-driving played a role doubled from five per cent in 2014 to 10 per cent in 2023.

While drug-driving convictions rose by 13.5 per cent to 27,000 in 2024, the number of drivers convicted of drink-driving offences fell by six per cent to 36,415.

Meanwhile, injuries from drink-driving incidents have significantly decreased since 1980, from around 20,000 annually to about 5,000 since 2020.

Despite this, the Government’s new road strategy proposes “taking tougher action on drink-driving” by reducing the legal limit of 80mg of alcohol per 100ml of blood to 50mg, or around a pint.

It would be the most significant reform to road safety laws since 1967, when the blood alcohol limit was first introduced.

Chris Philp, the shadow home secretary, said: “Labour are now proposing even more measures that will endanger country pubs.

“At the same time, the Government is completely failing to do more to address a more rapidly growing road safety issue – drug-driving. More drivers killed in a collision had drugs in their system than alcohol.

“The Government should prioritise toughening up on drug-drivers above measures which will strangle struggling country pubs.”

The British Beer and Pub Association warned that any toughening of measures on drink-driving would harm rural pubs in areas without public transport or reliable taxi services.

A spokesman said: “The pub sector continues to face huge challenges, so any additional policy measures that further impact trade will be of real concern to licensees, especially those in rural areas.”

‘Geographical lottery’

Drug-drivers face similar penalties as those caught drinking, including a minimum 12-month driving ban and up to six months in prison for serious or repeat offences.

Limits for illegal drugs such as cannabis, cocaine, ketamine and heroin are set at extremely low levels, but not at zero in order to account for accidental exposure.

However, Government-funded research has suggested that dangerous drug-drivers have been escaping prosecution – and putting lives at risk – because some police forces ration the number of testing kits issued to officers to just one a day.

The study, by the Parliamentary Advisory Council for Transport Safety, found there was a “geographical lottery” where the best-performing forces were catching 10 times more drug-drivers per head of population than the worst.

Drivers can also escape justice because of delays of four to five months in processing blood tests. Officers have only six months to prosecute.

Penalties to be reviewed

The Government’s new road safety strategy proposes that there should be a review of penalties and mandatory training for drink and drug-driving offences.

It has also pledged to explore alternative processing and evidence collection for drug-driving to “improve speed of results, supporting more robust enforcement outcomes.”

A DfT spokesman said the strategy would “save thousands of lives by targeting the root causes of deaths, including the impact of both alcohol and drugs”.

They added: “We’re determined to crack down on drug-driving, and the strategy includes new measures to modernise how we tackle it, including new testing methods, and powers to suspend driving licenses for those caught under the influence.

“We do not expect the new limit to harm pubs – experience in Scotland shows such changes have minimal impact on local businesses while making roads safer.”

Government sources also denied that the strategy failed to focus on tackling drug-driving, pointing to plans for roadside tests to catch drug-drivers to be stepped up.

Motorists suspected of driving under the influence of drugs would also have their licences suspended immediately under the plans. Roadside tests to catch drug-drivers will also be stepped up.

Police can already use roadside tests as a preliminary check, but results must be confirmed by requiring the driver to take a blood test in a lab. The strategy will set out plans to allow the police to charge suspects based solely on a roadside test.

However, ministers accept that this power will only be used when more reliable tests, that can assess exact amounts of drugs in people’s blood, are available. They also want to encourage police to use roadside tests more widely.

data.title: One in four electric cars sold in Britain are Chinese
data.description:
More than one in four electric vehicles (EVs) sold in the UK are now made in China, as Beijing helps to power Britain’s shift away from petrol and diesel.

Chinese-made vehicles accounted for 27.9pc of the more than 470,000 electric cars sold in the UK last year, according to the Society of Motor Manufacturers and Traders (SMMT).

Across all fuel types, Chinese-made vehicles accounted for a record 13.5pc of the total market last year, or one in eight. Sales rose by more than 50pc amid an aggressive push by brands including BYD, Jaecoo and Omoda to break into the UK.

Sales of BYD cars grew more than fivefold in Britain last year. The Chinese giant overtook Tesla to become the world’s biggest seller of electric cars in 2025.

Even celebrated British brands such as MG now count as made in China after falling under foreign ownership. Sweden-headquartered EV brand Polestar also manufacture in the country, while some Tesla models are made at the company’s Shanghai plant.

The surge in Chinese EV sales helped electric vehicles to account for 23.4pc of new cars registered in Britain in 2025, rising to 32.3pc in December, the SMMT said.

The figure will be welcomed by Labour ministers, who have vowed to ban the sale of new petrol and diesel cars by 2030 and hybrids by 2035.

However, the growing reliance on China for EVs is likely to prove controversial. Both the European Union and US have cracked down on imports from China, amid concerns about unfair subsidies and possible national security threats.

The US-based Centre for Strategic and International Studies has estimated that the Chinese government spent at least $230bn (£170bn) between 2009 to 2023 supporting its EV industry.

A 100pc tariff on Chinese electric vehicles in the US has effectively closed the market, while the EU has imposed heavy tariffs on imports.

The UK Government has said it has no plans for tariffs on Chinese car imports.

When combined with hybrid vehicles, battery-powered cars now account for just under half of all new car sales, the SMMT said. Plug-in hybrids, which combine a smaller battery with a petrol engine, are the fastest-growing part of the market, rising by 35pc last year.

Sales of fully electric cars were up 24pc, while petrol and diesel cars fell 8pc and 15pc respectively.

However, sales of EVs remained behind the zero emission vehicle mandate, which required 28pc of cars sold in 2025 to be electric.

This means sales were even further behind target than in 2024, when EVs made up 19.6pc of sales compared to a 22pc target. The mandate will rise again this year to a third of total car sales.

Manufacturers who miss the quota can cover the difference by buying credits from those who surpass it, making up sales in later years, or by cutting the CO2 emissions of other cars on the road.

However, manufacturers that are significantly below-target face the prospect of paying a fee of £12,000 per non-compliant car sold.

The SMMT said that manufacturers spent £5.5bn last year on subsidising EV sales in order to come closer to the target, or £11,000 per vehicle. The trade body said this was “unsustainable” and urged ministers to ease the requirements.

“The mandate still challenges the industry to hit levels that are apart from the natural demand that’s there,” said Mike Hawes, the SMMT’s chief executive.

He said a review of the mandate scheduled for 2027 should be brought forward to this year. “The assumptions behind the mandate when they were made, we need to review them.”

The EU has delayed plans to ban combustion engine cars, moving the date from 2035 to 2040. Labour has resisted calls to follow suit.

Overall, sales of new cars rose 3.5pc to 2.02m last year, the highest level since 2019, although they remain some way below pre-Covid levels.

data.title: US billionaires fleeing Trump snap up cut-price London mansions
data.description:
Billionaires fleeing Donald Trump’s America are snapping up cut-price London mansions from non-doms.

American buyers were behind one in five luxury home sales in London last year and are expected to continue to be a significant driver of purchases in 2026, according to Beauchamp Estates’ annual Billionaire Buyers in London report.

Gary Hersham, the founder of Beauchamp Estates, told The Telegraph that American buyers had become the “most serious” group in the £15m-plus market, with many “disillusioned” by Mr Trump’s presidency.

“I think it impacts the way Americans are thinking,” he said. “A lot of them want to leave the US and come to England.”

High-profile Trump exiles include TV host Ellen DeGeneres, who has said she would live in the Cotswolds after his re-election as president.

George Lucas, the Star Wars creator, last year spent £40m on a mansion in St John’s Wood, prompting speculation that he too may be relocating for political reasons.

Last year, 41 homes priced above £15m sold in London. Around 65pc of sales were non-doms quitting Britain for lower-tax destinations such as Dubai and Monaco, according to Beauchamp.

‘It does not match what you can get in London’

That exodus has put downward pressure on prices, and Mr Hersham said US buyers were also attracted by the value on offer in London.

He said: “If you look at what they’ve bought in London, the quality is exceptional compared with America; it’s a pittance compared to their wealth. Americans actually really like London and realise it’s a very good idea to diversify their assets.”

The gap between the average asking price and sale price widened to 7.6pc last year, compared to 7.1pc in 2024 and 5.6pc in 2023.

Mr Hersham added: “If you look at what you can buy for your money in Los Angeles or in New York in terms of style and quality, it does not match what you can get in London.”

Beauchamp Estates predicted that mansion prices will weaken by between 2pc and 3pc this year. Mr Hersham said some wealthy buyers continued to “view London with some caution” following recent tax and regulatory changes.

Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, unveiled a mansion tax on properties worth more than £2m in her November Budget. Ms Reeves has also set out plans to end non-dom status.

While Trump exiles are a significant part of the luxury London property market, Middle Eastern buyers are likely to continue to account for most activity this year, according to Beauchamp.

Gulf investors who have recently bought mansions in London include Abu Dhabi billionaire Hussain Sajwani, who bought a townhouse overlooking Hyde Park for £28m in spring last year.

data.title: Royals fly equivalent of twice around the world in helicopters
data.description:
Two new royal helicopters that came into service earlier this year have already flown a total of 58,000 miles – the equivalent of travelling twice around the Earth.

The AgustaWestland AW139s, used by senior members of the Royal family in the UK, racked up 420 hours of flying time between Feb 25 and the end of November.

The Princess Royal, who consistently carries out many public engagements all year, appears to have made the most use of the new aircraft, with her Gatcombe Park home in Gloucestershire the most frequently visited destination with 68 stops, according to flight data.

The Prince of Wales, who has made the environment a key plank of his work, has also proved an enthusiastic user of the new fleet, with 47 stops made in Windsor and a further five at Forest Lodge, the family home he moved into in October.

The data suggests the Prince flew by helicopter to multiple jobs each month, including a referee training course in Walsall, a women’s rugby match in Exeter, a visit to the England women’s football team at their training base in Staffordshire, a Homewards job in Sheffield and an Earthshot job in Norwich.

On Nov 25, one royal helicopter flew just under 500 miles, from its RAF Odiham base to Forest Lodge, before taking the Prince to his first stop in Wales, then onto Hawarden Airport, then Llandudno South before flying back to Forest Lodge and back to base.

When the Princess of Wales visited a wellbeing garden at Colchester Hospital on July 2, her helicopter made seven separate journeys totalling 390 miles: from its base at RAF Odiham to Windsor, then on to Merville Barracks in Colchester and from Merville Barracks to Anmer Hall, the Prince and Princess’s private residence in Norfolk.

After what appears to have been a refuelling stop at RAF Marham, it flew back to Windsor two and a half hours later, and finally, on to its base at Odiham.

On Armistice Day, when the Princess laid a wreath at the National Memorial Arboretum in Staffordshire, the helicopter flew from RAF Odiham to Forest Lodge, then onto Staffordshire and back the way it had come.

The Prince and Princess together travelled by helicopter to various joint engagements, including one at the Principality Stadium, Cardiff, where they watched a Six Nations match, one in Southport, where they met families bereaved in the 2024 knife attack and a visit to the Scottish islands in April.

The Prince also makes a point of travelling by public train, and is often photographed in his seat or at the station.

He travelled to Birmingham by train in January last year to attend a paramedic conference and to Liverpool, where he chatted to passengers in a first-class carriage.

The following month, he went to Poole, Dorset, by train for a Homewards job. On Feb 26, the Prince and Princess travelled to Pontypridd by train to celebrate St David’s Day.

Buckingham Palace has said that the King is “pretty allergic” to travelling by helicopter and would always “raise an eyebrow” and object when the mode of transport was suggested.

It was said in 2022 that such aircraft were considered only if every other option failed, but sources acknowledge that the position has changed.

It was announced in June 2024 that the Royal household was to take delivery of two new leased helicopters to replace two 15-year-old Sikorsky aircraft and was committed to maximising their use.

Aides said they would help reduce reliance on expensive fixed-wing travel and increase the household’s ability to utilise Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF) as they operate with the maximum industry standard of 50 per cent.

Tim Fauchon, the chief executive of the British Helicopter Association (BHA), said the King was “leading by example” when it came to using the greenest forms of aviation.

Explaining that the King’s flight crew are members of the BHA, Mr Fauchon said it was common knowledge among the association that the flight “actively seeks out” airports which can supply SAF for its operations.

“I know full well the Royal Flight do look for a blend,” he added.

“You’ve got to appreciate it comes as an expense... but the helicopter industry is very forward-leaning on doing the (regulatory) clearances for SAF.”

Flight data analysed by The Telegraph reveals that between them, the two new aircraft made around 340 trips over nine months, if journeys with stops of one hour or more are treated as separate trips.

If this stopping time is reduced to ten minutes or less, the number of trips rises to around 700.

The choppers made 45 stops at Kensington Palace, 32 at Bagshot Park, home to the Duke and Duchess of Edinburgh and 14 at Holyrood Palace in Edinburgh.

There were 14 stops in Lacock, Wiltshire, near the Queen’s private home, 11 at Sandringham, six at Dumfries House, home to the King’s Foundation, and five at Buckingham Palace.

Since they came into use on Feb 25, one or the other flew on 142 out of the 279 days until Nov 30 – equivalent to almost every other day.

The longest individual leg by distance was a 372-mile flight from Dundee back to the RAF Odiham base on May 23. Over the previous two days, the chopper had been bouncing between various Scottish locations, including Dumfries House, the headquarters of the King’s Foundation, in Ayrshire.

The furthest distance travelled in a single day was 941 miles, when one of the helicopters shuttled from Leuchars in Scotland over to Campbeltown, then back down to RAF Odiham via Prestwick and Edinburgh, on July 3.

On that day, the King was carrying out various engagements celebrating Scottish culture and community as part of the annual Holyrood Week.

The shortest trip took place when the Princess Royal was visiting the Channel Islands on May 10.

One of the helicopters flew from Guernsey to Sark, first landing in a small field to its south before then taking off again and flying just 1.9 miles to the centre of the island. 

The aircraft landed less than seven minutes later in a field, for just over three hours, before taking the Princess back to her Gatcombe Park home.

The cost of royal travel is covered by the Sovereign Grant, an annual payment to support official duties, which is pegged to the profits from the Crown estate.

In 2025-26, the grant increased to £132.1m from £86.3m the previous year.

An aide said last year that helicopters were “a very important tool”, allowing the King and other members of the family to be “seen throughout the length and breadth of the UK”.

Asked whether helicopter use should be reduced, the official said: “If you’re going to make an investment of that magnitude, then you need to sweat the asset.

“So, therefore, you need to maximise the utilisation of the aircraft otherwise you end up in a situation whereby a small number of visits, compared to the cost of running those assets, it does not make good financial sense.”

The annual Sovereign Grant details only journeys that cost £17,000 or more. The most recent report, published in June, listed only four such journeys in the last financial year, wrapping the cost in with linked charter flights.

But it revealed that a further 141 helicopter journeys were made over the 12 months, costing £475,290.

The previous year, no flights costing more than £17,000 were declared but 170 helicopter journeys were taken, at a cost of almost £1.1m.

A Buckingham Palace spokesman said: “Helicopters are a key component of the royal travel plan due to their unique capability to access remote regions of the UK which are not otherwise readily served by other modes of transport. 

“They also allow members of the Royal family to undertake multiple engagements in a given day.”

data.title: Steve Reed lines up administrators for Thames Water
data.description:
Ministers have appointed City insolvency advisers to prepare for the potential collapse of Thames Water.

Steve Reed, the Environment Secretary, has selected FTI Consulting to help draw up contingency plans in the event that the struggling utility giant is placed into a taxpayer-backed special administration regime.

It comes as Thames Water, which has 16 million customers across London and the South East, teeters on the brink of collapse as it struggles under a £17bn debt pile.

Its future was cast into fresh doubt in June after private equity firm KKR pulled out of a £4bn rescue deal.

The company’s creditors have put forward an alternative £17bn rescue plan, but the bailout is contingent on regulators agreeing to waive historical fines.

Mr Reed has previously suggested this would not happen, making a government intervention appear increasingly likely. It is estimated that a state bailout would cost the taxpayer as much as £4bn.

The Environment Secretary in June said Labour was stepping up preparations to temporarily nationalise Thames Water. A government source said the move was a natural consequence of this.

The move to appoint FTI, which was first reported by Sky News, places the consulting firm in prime position to act as the company’s administrator should it collapse into insolvency, though the appointment of a special administrator is ultimately a decision for the court.

A Government spokesman said: “The Government will always act in the national interest on these issues.

“The company remains financially stable, but we have stepped up our preparations and stand ready for all eventualities, including applying for a special administration regime if that were to become necessary.”

Thames is currently locked in discussions with its creditors and regulator Ofwat over a new rescue deal for the company.

Creditors have been pushing for ministers to intervene to wipe out historical fines, saying a “regulatory reset” was required to ensure the utility is financially sustainable.

Thames was handed a record £123m fine in May and creditors say it could be in line for penalties of more than £1bn relating to environmental and pollution failings.

The £17bn rescue plan involves injecting £5bn in new equity and debt and writing off billions of pounds in existing debt.

A Thames Water spokesman said: “Our focus remains on a holistic and fundamental recapitalisation, delivering a market-led solution which includes targeting investment grade credit ratings and returning the company to a stable financial foundation.

“Constructive discussions with our many stakeholders continue.”

data.title: Miliband staff in conflict of interest row over National Grid shares
data.description:
Staff at Ed Miliband’s energy quango own shares in the company responsible for rewiring Britain for net zero, raising concerns over a potential conflict of interest.

The National Energy System Operator (Neso) was hived off from the National Grid in October 2024 and tasked with overseeing the electricity operator on behalf of billpayers.

However, in the wake of the deal, staff were given six months to buy discounted shares in National Grid, which is listed on the London Stock Exchange and generates billions of pounds in profits each year.

This means some employees at Neso, which is an independent non-profit, stand to gain financially from National Grid while overseeing its operations and future investments.

This has raised concerns over a potential conflict of interest at Neso, as such decisions could boost National Grid’s revenues and in turn its share price.

Richard Tice, Reform UK’s energy spokesman, said it was wrong for Neso employees to have a financial interest in the companies they oversee.

He said: “The Neso regulator’s staff could be financially rewarded for being a softer touch with the entity they regulate. They used to call that a conflict of interest.

“A Reform government would remove the net zero obligations from Neso’s remit and enforce a total reset of its objectives, focused on secure, plentiful, reliable, cheap electricity.”

National Grid, which is worth £57bn, is integral to Mr Miliband’s goal of generating 95pc of electricity from clean sources such as wind and solar. This is because it is responsible for building the cables and pylons required to transmit green power across the UK.

Neso confirmed that staff have been allowed to retain their shares in National Grid but stressed that they would be subject to a declaration process under Neso’s share ownership policy.

They refused to say how many of its 2,000 employees still own shares in National Grid.

Kathryn Porter, an energy analyst, urged Neso to come clean: “It’s completely inappropriate for staff to own these shares. They should be sold and staff made whole for any loss arising from the forced sale.

“This position is completely untenable. Declare the extent of the share ownership issue and start a divestment process.”

Shares in National Grid have increased by more than 20pc over the past 12 months, with investors rallying behind the company, owing to increased demand for renewables.

National Grid is the largest of three monopolies in charge of the UK’s transmission system, alongside Scottish Power and SSE.

All three are now tasked with spending tens of billions of pounds on new electricity infrastructure to help hit net zero, aimed at reducing the country’s reliance on fossil fuels such as gas.

‘Very strict’ ownership policy

National Grid, in particular, is about to embark on a £90bn expansion plan by upgrading the nation’s high-voltage power lines, substations and other transmission infrastructure.

Neso was bought by the Labour Government last year for £630m to remove any perceived conflicts of interest with National Grid.

For staff, however, the move to Neso meant they lost access to discounted National Grid shares, a highly valued perk.

A compromise was then reached under which staff moving to Neso could retain the right to buy shares for another six months – a scheme that ended last March.

They were also allowed to keep shares already held.

Members of Neso’s leadership team who moved from National Grid were obliged to sell all shares but were compensated with a lucrative long-term bonus scheme.

Fintan Slye, Neso’s chief executive, was paid an annual salary of £288,000, with a bonus of £275,400, plus other benefits that took his total remuneration to £774,000.

A Neso spokesman said: “Whilst a small minority of Neso staff retain minimal shares in National Grid, they are subject to very strict energy share ownership policy rules, underpinned by law, which requires permission if they ever want to sell, to ensure the highest standards of propriety and ethics.”

Neso is constituted as a private company controlled by the Energy Secretary, and so is not covered by the UK Corporate Governance Code or the Wates Principles on managing public companies.

This means its directors are not subject to public scrutiny or annual re-election, and its staff are not civil servants – although they are expected to follow government codes of conduct.

A National Grid spokesman confirmed that shares had been offered to Neso staff at a 20pc discount for six months but that the scheme was now closed.

They added: “If an employee leaves due to injury, disability, redundancy or a business sale such as Neso, they may exercise their Sharesave (SAYE) options. This must be done within six months of the date they cease employment.

“Any Neso colleagues who hold shares in National Grid are subject to very strict energy share ownership policy rules, underpinned by law.”

data.title: Treasury doesn’t understand risks of shadow banking bubble, Lords warn
data.description:
Treasury ministers have come under fire from an influential group of peers for failing to grasp the risks of Wall Street’s shadow banking bubble.

On Thursday, the House of Lords’ financial services regulation committee heavily criticised the Government for having “demonstrated a limited grasp” of the risks posed to the financial system from the private markets boom.

The report said the Treasury had an attitude of “passivity”, which was all the more surprising given the potential risks to the UK’s financial stability from shadow banking and private markets.

The rapid growth of private credit, which is corporate loans made by non-bank lenders, has raised concerns about its stability and the potential impact of a crash on the wider financial system.

Concerns about weakening lending standards were fuelled last year by the collapse of two US companies – First Brands, a car parts supplier, and Tricolor, a subprime auto lender.

Jamie Dimon, the head of JPMorgan, warned that more “cockroaches” would emerge because of lax lending standards over the years.

Lord Hollick, a member of the committee and former boss of media giant UBM, said: “The Treasury itself needs to be curious about this and needs to be actively engaged in looking for this and finding this information and giving us their view because obviously a lot of savers’ money, pension funds and insurance companies are going to be tied up with this.

“Heaven forbid that anything should go wrong, but clearly it’s of sufficient size and importance for the Treasury also to take a careful look at it.”

While the Lords committee praised the work of the Bank of England, led by Andrew Bailey, it warned that the Treasury appeared to defer to the Bank, with limited engagement with the issue itself.

The report concluded that there was not enough information to conclude whether private markets were “systemic” – in other words, whether they could pose a risk to the wider financial system.

In particular, it praised the Bank for its plan to conduct the world’s first “stress test” of the shadow banking sector to see how it would cope with a global shock.

The exercise will run over two rounds, with results expected to be published in early 2027.

While tougher regulations for banks following the financial crisis have boosted their resilience, they have also pushed some of their traditional lending activities outside the more regulated banking sector.

Britain’s private market has grown by 56pc since 2015 to $185bn (£138bn) and is the second-largest after the United States, the report said.

A Treasury spokesman said: “We have worked together with the regulators to significantly increase our focus of non-banks sectors in recent years and have a robust, flexible framework to protect financial stability.

“We are considering the committee’s recommendations and will respond in due course.”

data.title: Starmer’s AI drive ‘will lead to hosepipe bans’
data.description:
Sir Keir Starmer’s artificial intelligence (AI) plans risk leading to hosepipe bans and could drive up water bills, a government advisory body has warned.

A report from the Government Digital Sustainability Alliance (GDSA) said that placing major data centres in water-stressed areas could lead to “increased water bills [and] rationing”, adding it risked “potentially exacerbating social conflicts”.

Last week, the Prime Minister trumpeted a series of promised investments in AI data centres, including the construction of Britain’s most powerful AI supercomputer and a new “AI growth zone” in Northumberland.

The string of investments by US tech giants, which coincided with Donald Trump’s state visit, led to predictions of a “big bang” for the British AI industry.

However, the GDSA, a group set up by the Department for Environment, Food & Rural Affairs, claimed that Britain’s water supplies were not adequately prepared for a boom in data centres, which can require large quantities of water to cool computer systems.

It warned that the tech projects were a “blind spot” in official water forecasting, and that utilities were not planning for a surge in data centre infrastructure.

“The siting of water-intensive infrastructure in already water-stressed areas, particularly those with existing socio-economic vulnerabilities, raises significant environmental justice concerns,” it said.

“This practice could disproportionately impact local communities, potentially leading to increased water bills, rationing or reduced environmental flows.”

It warned there was a conflict between the Government’s AI dreams and Britain’s ability to manage water supplies.

“The rapid expansion of AI presents a profound challenge to the UK’s water security and environmental sustainability,” it said.

“The evidence demonstrates that AI’s substantial water footprint, primarily driven by data centre cooling and energy generation, is escalating at an alarming rate and is largely unaccounted for in current national water resource planning.

“This critical oversight, coupled with existing and projected water deficits in the UK, creates a significant risk of exacerbating water stress, leading to potential social and environmental conflicts.”

Source of conflict

The country is already facing pressure on its future water supply. England will require an extra five billion litres of water a day by 2050, according to the Environment Agency.

The GDSA, which is made up of business executives, academics and industry groups, exists to give the Government advice when developing new policies. Its members views do not necessarily reflect those of the Government.

The report by Chris Kenny, the co-chair of one of the GDSA’s panel groups, said measures recently proposed by water companies to improve Britain’s water supplies by 2050 did not account for the growth in data centres.

Data centres are crucial to AI and use large amounts of energy. Last week, Microsoft pledged to invest £22bn in Britain, the majority of which will go towards new infrastructure.

Thames Water warned that its water supplies risked being stressed by more than 100 new data centres planned in the regions it covers.

The tech industry has disputed that data centres risk adding to Britain’s water problems. Industry body techUK said in a report last month that the majority of new data centres used so-called closed loop-cooling systems that do not require any external water supplies.

Plans to support the AI industry have led to other conflicts within the Government.

Peter Kyle, the Business Secretary, has championed a proposed giant data centre on Teesside that would conflict with a proposed hydrogen plant believed to be supported by Ed Miliband, the Energy Secretary.

A government spokesman said: “Data centres are part of the vital infrastructure behind the digital technology that powers our businesses and everyday lives, and we need substantial investment to harness AI’s transformative potential.

“The planning system takes into account water scarcity and local plans themselves should take into account the long-term implications for water supply. We’re also unlocking £104bn in water infrastructure investment, which includes improving water supplies around data centres.”

data.title: Thames Water lenders prepared to take £7bn hit to avoid nationalisation
data.description:
A group of Thames Water creditors is prepared to effectively write off £7.5bn of debt in a bid to avoid nationalisation.

The consortium has proposed a turnaround plan that would cancel around a third of Thames Water’s near-£20bn debt pile and commit the group to investing in the company.

Outstanding fines would be paid – but the investor group, dubbed London & Valley Water, would also seek leniency on future penalties in exchange for investment.

Thames Water revealed over the summer that annual losses had hit £1.6bn as a soaring debt burden and continued sewage failures fuelled its financial crisis.

Bosses said it would take at least a decade to turn around Britain’s largest water company.

The company’s creditors are looking to secure backing for their plans to avoid the utility being put into a temporary special administration regime, which would effectively wipe out their investments.

Lenders said they would not pay a dividend while conducting their turnaround plans or until the company is listed.

They also committed not to sell the business before the end of March 2030.

The consortium said the “more ambitious” plan would increase investment in Thames Water by around £1bn compared to proposals they submitted to Ofwat in May.

The latest proposal was the result of “three months of detailed discussion” with the water regulator and would “support long-term financial resilience and is intended to help the company pay for outstanding fines”, it added.

Mike McTighe, a City veteran whose current roles include chairman of Telegraph Media Group, would become chairman of Thames Water under the creditors’ proposals.

He said: “There is a huge amount of work to be done to turn around Thames Water and deliver the improved service and environmental outcomes that customers and local communities deserve.

“From day one, we will inject billions in new investment, strengthen Thames Water’s balance sheet, transform the company for thousands of hard-working frontline staff and begin the delivery of an operational turnaround that puts 16 million customers and the environment first.”

Lenders of Thames Water’s “Class A” debt would receive at least 10pc of new equity in the utility company in exchange for writing off about 25pc of their stake, worth around £4bn.

Thames Water said it remains in discussions over the bid but any agreement would be subject to High Court approval.

Chris Weston, the chief executive of Thames Water, said: “Today is an important milestone in our ongoing work with creditors and stakeholders to secure a market-led recapitalisation that establishes the financial and regulatory foundations required to support the investment and performance improvements our customers expect.”

data.title: ‘How will my £100 Nationwide bonus be taxed?’
data.description:
Email your tax questions to Mike at: taxhacks@telegraph.co.uk*

Dear Mike,

Nationwide advise that the members’ Fairer Share scheme, which currently pays £100 per annum, is from a distribution of profits.

On that basis, I would regard the payment as a form of dividend, and should be taxed as such.

However, HMRC appears to classify this as an interest payment.

My situation is that I use up the £1,000 savings interest allowance and so this £100 was taxed at 20pc, but I don’t use up the £500 dividend allowance (and if I did the tax rate would be 8.75pc).

Your view would be appreciated.

Regards,

– Shaun

Dear Shaun,

I understand entirely your frustration and why you think the distribution should be treated as a dividend. After all, if you hold shares in a bank that distributes money to their members in this way, it would be classified as a dividend.

The short answer to your question is that HMRC is correct to show it as interest, because – as I will explain – Nationwide is a building society rather than a bank.

Building societies have a long and proud history as locally-based mutual societies owned by their members. The first recorded society was in Birmingham in 1775, and by 1910 there were over 1,700 societies.

Everything changed in 1986 when the Building Societies Act introduced greater freedoms, including the ability to convert into a bank.

The first to do so was the Abbey National in 1989. The Cheltenham and Gloucester building society, along with many others, followed in 1995 when it was taken over by Lloyds Bank.

Nationwide, in contrast, has remained a mutual society. It is now the largest mutual building society in the UK and indeed the world. Others that have remained as mutuals include the Coventry, Yorkshire, Skipton and Leeds building societies.

Nationwide is a well-run and profitable society, which decided to reward eligible loyal members with the £100 “Fairer Share” payment you mention in 2025 – for the third year in a row – as well as a one-off £50 “Big Nationwide Thank You” payment.

This was paid in the current tax year between April 9-30 2025, to 12 million members following the Nationwide acquisition of Virgin Money.

There were detailed qualifying conditions for these bonuses dependent on the holding of current accounts, savings accounts or mortgages, and also subject to set transaction and balance criteria.

If Nationwide had been a bank these payments would have been taxed as a dividend. However, as a mutual society different rules apply. These are helpfully set out in the HMRC manuals at CTM40513 and CTM40530.

These include the statement “any sum paid by way of dividend, bonus, interest or otherwise, that is paid to a shareholder in a registered society, and is paid by reference to the shareholder’s holding in the society’s capital... are treated as interest”. As the manual explains, this treatment is not a matter of choice by Nationwide, nor by HMRC, but set by legal statute.

Nationwide is obliged to report the details of all payments made to HMRC. If you are in self-assessment, you should nevertheless add this to any other gross interest you receive outside an Isa.

If you have PAYE income, this may have already shown up on your tax code. If not, it will be reflected in the tax reconciliation after the end of the tax year. There was no tax deducted on these payments and they are therefore part of your untaxed savings income for reporting purposes.

In many cases, this payment will have fallen within the savings allowance, although I appreciate, Shaun, that your savings allowance has already been utilised.

Rather confusingly, this tax treatment differs from the way payments were treated when building societies either converted into banks or were acquired by them.

In these cases, the payments received by members were treated as capital receipts on which capital gains tax may have applied. That will have depended on whether the payments were covered by your available annual CGT exemption at the time. It might also have depended on whether you received the payment in cash.

Many building societies, such as the Cheltenham and Gloucester, gave a loan note alternative. This allowed members to roll over their gains into loan notes and redeem them in following years within the annual capital gains tax exemptions available. In addition, it allowed loan notes to be given to spouses tax-free and for them likewise to spread the gain over several tax years.

Finally, although this is not my specialist area, I believe that both the £50 and £100 payments are treated as capital for the purpose of calculating an entitlement to certain benefits, such as Universal Credit and income support. 

If the sums received take you over the £6,000 threshold, you should report this to the Department for Work and Pensions or your local council for Housing Benefit Council Tax Support.

Note, however, that different rules apply for pensioners where the lower capital limit is £10,000 and there is no upper capital limit if you receive the guarantee credit part of pension credit.

Mike Warburton was previously a tax director with accountants Grant Thornton and is now retired. His columns should not be taken as advice, or as a personal recommendation, but as a starting point for readers to undertake their own further research.

*Please note that by submitting your content to us, you are consenting to The Telegraph processing your personal data where required by law. For further details, please see our Privacy Notice.

data.title: Miliband net zero plans hit by surging reliance on gas
data.description:
Britain’s reliance on fossil fuels has increased for the first time in four years, dealing a major blow to Ed Miliband’s hopes of decarbonising the grid by 2030.

Gas-fired power plants generated 26.8pc of power in 2025, a rise of 1.1pc from the year prior, according to new figures from the Government’s National Energy System Operator (Neso).

This stemmed largely from reduced nuclear output across Britain, as the country’s nine remaining reactors were hit by technical faults and unplanned blackouts.

The latest figures also revealed that the UK relied on imports for up to 14pc of its electricity last year, with London and the South East frequently powered by energy from France.

Increased reliance on fossil fuels and continued imports from overseas will raise fresh scrutiny over the Energy Secretary’s goal of attaining clean power by 2030.

To achieve this, Mr Miliband has vowed to spend billions of pounds on intermittent renewables such as wind and solar farms, with the cost of this expansion set to be passed through to households and businesses.

Claire Coutinho, the shadow energy secretary, has accused Mr Miliband of needlessly driving up bills and putting the country’s energy security at risk.

She said: “What this data shows is that we need reliable, 24/7 power for when the wind doesn’t blow and the sun doesn’t shine – and that means more nuclear and more gas.

“Ed Miliband’s plan to build more wind and solar farms than ever before will do nothing to power our economy on a cold, windless, cloudy day.

“His ideological approach to energy policy means British families are going to continue to see their bills rise, jobs lost overseas, and the risk of blackouts increase.”

The Energy Department hit back by blaming the previous government for “years of dither and delay on new nuclear”, with a spokesman claiming that this left the country reliant on power plants dating back to the 1960s.

Total nuclear output fell from 13.7pc of UK generation in 2024 to 11.8pc last year, the latest Neso figures show, with a string of plant failures to blame.

This month, the Hartlepool nuclear power station was forced to shut down both its reactors for unplanned repairs, while Heysham 2 and Torness have also scaled back output.

EDF, the French power firm that operates the UK’s remaining reactors, has already closed several, while the rest have been kept in operation beyond their original lifespans.

However, all but Sizewell B will have to close by March 2030, making it crucial for Hinckley Point C, the proposed nuclear power station in Somerset, to be ready by that point.

Meanwhile, the latest data reveal a steady rise in low-carbon output in recent years, with wind and solar contributing 36.5pc to total power generation in 2025, up from 24pc in 2019.

The planned expansion of green energy under Labour means this figure is set to rise further, with plans for hundreds of new wind, solar and battery plants across England.

The revelations come just a week before Mr Miliband is due to announce the latest round of renewables contracts, under which developers will get guaranteed minimum prices for the power they generate.

The cost of those subsidies will be added to energy bills, with wind and solar poised to be the biggest beneficiaries.

However, some analysts warn that the expansion of renewables may not be enough to help decarbonise the grid in time.

Adam Berman, of Energy UK, which represents the supply industry, said Mr Miliband’s target of decarbonising the grid by 2030 should be seen as an aspiration rather than being set in stone.

“There may be a blip upwards now but the long-term trend is clear,” he said.

“Gas-fired power generation produced about 39pc of electricity back 2019 and now its down to 25pc. The Clean Power 2030 target really has focused minds in government in a way that was not the case before.”

A spokesman for the Energy Department said: “These [Neso] statistics reflect decisions made by previous governments, including years of dither and delay on new nuclear.

“This Government is turning this around, already consenting record amounts of solar – enough for the equivalent of 8.5 million homes. These projects take time to build, but we are making the decisions to benefit Britain for years to come.”

Ana Musat, of Renewable UK, the wind industry’s trade body, said: “The UK’s wind and solar farms generated a record amount of clean power last year, but as our nuclear fleet is retiring we’ve been forced to burn more gas, making it even more important to speed up the delivery of more clean power.

“We expect new generation records to be set this year with a huge pipeline of renewable energy projects under construction or being planned. We’re working closely with the Government to ensure that achieve clean power by 2030, and next week we’re hoping to secure a record amount of new offshore wind capacity in the next auction for projects to go ahead.”

data.title: LBC owner’s losses hit £150m as loan costs rise
data.description:
The media company behind LBC and The News Agents podcast has seen losses rise to £150m after borrowing costs wiped out growing revenues.

Pre-tax losses at Global Media & Entertainment, which also runs Capital and Heart, rose from £130m to £150m in the 12 months to March 2025, according to newly filed accounts.

The deterioration came even as revenues rose by £40m to £898m last year. While the business performed well, with underlying earnings before interest, tax and other costs up by 10pc to £155m, debt costs weighed on the business.

Borrowings rose to £1.9bn, including £1.65bn of loans from Global’s multimillionaire backers, with nearly £193m of interest added to the debt pile last year rather than paid in cash. Accumulated losses now stand at more than £1.4bn.

Global was founded by Ashley Tabor‑King, whose father provided the original £375m backing for the company in 2007. Michael Tabor, a former bookmaker, racehorse owner and property investor with an estimated net worth of £800m, retains significant control.

In the accounts, directors at Global highlighted the recent success of The News Agents, a current affairs show hosted by former BBC journalists Emily Maitlis, Jon Sopel and Lewis Goodall. The podcast achieved 100m downloads in 2025 and in December 2024 hosted a sold-out event in London’s Royal Albert Hall.

Global’s other podcasts include The Sports Agents, fronted by Gabby Logan and Mark Chapman, and My Therapist Ghosted Me.

Weekly listeners to Heart, meanwhile, rose by 992,000 to 13.4m, making it Britain’s biggest radio brand, surpassing BBC Radio 2. Sister brand Capital has also quietly overtaken Radio 1, with Global branding it as the country’s biggest hit music station for younger listeners.

The company’s accounts also praised “Capital Taylor’s Version” – a pop-up station, dedicated entirely to playing songs by Taylor Swift, and covering news about the singer’s Eras tour.

As well as commercial radio and podcasts, Global controls more than 235,000 billboard advertising sites across Britain, which it estimates reaches an estimated 95pc of the population. Adjusted profits in this part of the business rose 25pc year on year from £112m to about £140m.

In September 2024, the group was awarded contracts with Network Rail and Transport for London – the former giving Global control of the biggest roadside advertising network in the country. It also won exclusive rights to manage advertising on the Tube, Elizabeth Line and other networks in the capital.

In March, Stephen Miron stepped down as chief executive after 16 years at the helm and became chairman of Global. He was replaced by Simon Pitts, the former boss of Scottish broadcaster STV.

Mr Tabor-King was awarded a CBE for services to media and entertainment in 2024.

Global declined to comment.
