data.title: Thames Water’s rescue plan risks setting a dangerous precedent
data.description:
The latest version of a creditor-led rescue plan for Thames Water ticks multiple boxes. One can see why the company, ministers and the industry regulator might be minded to give it the green light in the coming weeks.

The pressure to hammer out a deal is intense. The threat of a multibillion-pound bill landing on Rachel Reeves’s desk before her Budget in November is concentrating Whitehall minds.

Thames is burning cash rapidly. A separate announcement from the company revealed that in August it called on the remaining £628m of a £1.5bn emergency loan.

It is further worrying evidence of the extent to which an organisation providing critical services to 13 million people continues to live hand-to-mouth after years of reckless ownership.

With the clock ticking, the “London and Valley” consortium of lenders has come up with a decent upgrade on the somewhat vague proposal published a month ago.

Then, the prospective new owners declared their undying commitment to “a new transparent and collaborative relationship with regulators”, before outlining a plan lacking in details.

The highlights of their latest attempt include a more generous portion of equity and a bigger debt write-off than previously envisaged. A commitment to not pay dividends for five years, along with the ambition of eventually returning Thames to the stock market are also welcome measures.

Yet all the hard work that has gone into answering many of the concerns of regulators, Government and long-suffering customers risks being overshadowed by what amounts to a fresh attempt to wriggle out of fines.

Having sensibly given up on trying to dodge outstanding penalties for pumping sewage into waterways, Thames’s wannabe owners are now trying to inoculate themselves against future punishment.

“The current regulatory framework for the water industry does not allow it to successfully turnaround its business,” London and Valley says.

The consortium won’t take “no” for an answer, it seems.

Steve Reed, the former environment secretary, told parliament in June that Thames must meet its statutory obligations, after being asked about possible “regulatory easements”.

But the very idea that an industry that treats Britain’s rivers as its dumping ground should face softer environmental targets – or “stretching but achievable incentives” in the consortium’s words – won’t wash.

It’s hard to square this with the consortium’s warm words.

Mike McTighe, the proposed future chairman of Thames – he is also chairman of the company behind The Telegraph – talks about “rebuilding public trust”.

The creditors themselves admit that Thames has presided over “a cycle of underinvestment and underperformance that has driven trust in Thames Water to an all-time low.”

Few would argue with that assessment. Yet any agreement that makes it easier for the company to pollute with greater impunity is an improbable basis for the restoration of Thames’s relationship with the public.

Consider that in an industry that has vandalised the environment, this is a chronic offender.

Thames’s record is getting worse, not better. Some 50pc more raw sewage was discharged into Britain’s waterways from Thames’s ageing system last year, compared with the previous 12 months.

In May, Thames Water was fined £123m by Ofwat – the biggest ever fine imposed by the regulator.

From a purely financial perspective, the creditors’ plea for clemency is understandable.

They expect future fines of more than £1bn if current regulations are applied and argue that something has to give if Thames is to receive the investment it needs.

This financial hole was of Thames’s own making, however. Its creditors bankrolled it as it hollowed itself out. To turn around now and demand that the rules are changed to make pumping effluent into rivers a less costly business is an affront to justice and risks setting a horrible precedent.

Perhaps even now, after months and months of negotiations, the hedge funds that swooped in to buy up debt on the cheap when Thames was on the brink, still don’t understand the extent of public and political anger.

Customer ire stretches to the whole gamut of misdeeds – rising bills, poor service, undeserved dividends and excessive executive pay.

But it is the epidemic of pollution that has attracted the most fury. That the creditors have chosen to attack on that front seems “courageous”, as they say in the Civil Service.

Indeed, I am willing to bet that the majority of people want to see stiffer fines, not softer ones, particularly if the current set-up has failed to act as a deterrent. Weaker rules would surely lead to more pollution, not less.

Meanwhile, the knock-on effect of granting Thames an easier ride would be that every other water company in Britain would be entitled to expect the same exemptions on the basis of fairness.

A reminder from the creditors that their offer is the only credible one left after private equity firm KKR abandoned its bid looks designed to focus minds. Ditto talks of how the plan “will avoid special administration”, and ensure that “Thames Water’s turnaround will not fall to the UK taxpayer”.

Decency, however, demands that Ofwat and the Government don’t allow themselves to be pushed around by a bunch of opportunistic hedge funds.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Millions of households to face higher water bills
data.description:
Millions of households are facing higher-than-expected water bills after the competition watchdog allowed five utility companies to increase their charges.

Following a provisional ruling by the Competition and Markets Authority (CMA), Anglian Water, Northumbrian Water, South East Water, Southern Water and Wessex Water will be able to increase bills by a collective £556m over the next five years.

While that is less than the £2.7bn requested by the companies, it will still lead to some households paying annual bills of up to £638 a year.

Emma Hardy, the minister for water and flooding, said she understood the public’s anger over bill rises, but expected companies to offer support to those struggling to pay.

Between them, Anglian and Northumbrian have been allowed to raise bills by a further 1pc, while Southern, South East and Wessex have been handed increases of 3pc, 4pc and 5pc, respectively.

Together, they serve around seven million customers across the UK.

The ruling means that Anglian will charge an average bill of £599, while Northumbrian bill payers will pay £495. South East Water will increase bills to £322, while Southern will charge £638.

Wessex was handed the biggest increase of 5pc, meaning its bills will rise to £622. All had asked for larger increases.

Appeal to the CMA

It comes after the companies appealed to the CMA earlier this year, requesting permission to increase their bills higher than Ofwat, the water regulator, had previously ruled.

In defence of its decision, the CMA said the extra money would help reduce pollution and strengthen supply, although it also said it would help cover the companies’ rising debt costs.

Kirstin Baker, who chaired an independent group of experts appointed by the CMA, said: “We’ve found that water companies’ requests for significant bill increases, on top of those allowed by Ofwat, are largely unjustified.

“We understand the real pressure on household budgets and have worked to keep increases to a minimum, while still ensuring there is funding to deliver essential improvements at a reasonable cost.”

Thames Water, Britain’s biggest water company, had also appealed, but its proceedings have been put on hold while it battles to stave off nationalisation.

Ms Hardy said efforts were being made to restore the public’s trust in water companies.

She said: “We’ve made sure that investment cash goes into infrastructure upgrades, not bonuses, and we’re creating a tough new regulator to clean up our waterways and restore trust in the system.”

It comes amid growing anger over the state of the water sector, which has been responsible for a rise in sewage failures in recent years.

Mike Keil, the chief executive of the Consumer Council for Water (CCW), said: “Many customers are still trying to absorb the impact of April’s unprecedented rise in water bills, so any further increases will be very unwelcome.

“CCW’s own analysis suggests there was an opportunity to reduce these five companies’ financing costs, cutting bills by around £41 a year, but instead the CMA has chosen to increase their rate of return.

“There is a danger the customers of these companies will end up paying more, without seeing any additional improvements in return.”

Joanna Elson, the chief executive of Independent Age, a charity, said higher bills would be “extremely damaging to people on low incomes living in these areas”.

She said: “The people in later life we support tell us the drastic lengths they are taking to reduce their water costs, including only washing a couple of times a week, not flushing the toilet and cutting down on laundry. This shouldn’t be happening in modern Britain.”

Ms Elson added: “We urge the UK Government to protect customers on low incomes from rising water bills by establishing a national water social tariff. This will end the postcode lottery.

 “It’s not right that your address dictates how much financial support you receive.”

data.title: Drug giant hit by Trump autism claims bought for record $49bn
data.description:
A US drug giant hit by Donald Trump’s claims linking its paracetamol brand to an increased risk of autism has been bought for $49bn (£37bn) after shares crashed to a record low.

On Monday, Kenvue, which makes the Tylenol paracetamol brand, said it had agreed to be acquired by consumer goods giant Kimberly-Clark, which owns Kleenex, Huggies and Andrex.

The takeover comes after Kenvue shares hit their lowest level on record, with $13bn wiped off the company since the start of the year amid heightened scrutiny over Mr Trump’s claims about Tylenol.

In September, the US president said pregnant women should “fight like hell” not to take the paracetamol drug, urging them to “tough it out” when in pain.

Mr Trump went on to claim that the use of Tylenol during pregnancy could make autism more likely, citing unfounded links. The US president said: “Don’t take Tylenol. Don’t take it.”

The claim prompted a backlash from doctors who argue that it puts pregnant women’s health at risk.

The Medicines and Healthcare products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) issued guidance saying there was “no evidence that taking paracetamol during pregnancy causes autism in children”.

In the UK, Wes Streeting, the Health Secretary, urged pregnant women not to pay “any attention whatsoever” to Mr Trump’s claims.

Kenvue said it “strongly disagrees with allegations that it does and is deeply concerned about the health risks and confusion this poses for expecting mothers and parents”.

Last week, Robert F Kennedy Jr, the US health secretary, also admitted that there was not “sufficient” evidence to link Tylenol to autism.

However, Kenvue is still facing a lawsuit in Texas over claims that it deceptively marketed its pain medication to pregnant people despite the alleged autism risk.

Kenvue’s $48.7bn takeover marks the largest buyout of a US consumer goods maker ever recorded, ahead of Mars’s $35.9bn takeover of Kellanova last year.

The offer price is at a 46pc premium to Kenvue’s closing price on Friday.

Kenvue, which was spun out of Johnson & Johnson in 2023, has also been drawn into court cases in the UK over its talc powder.

In October, a lawsuit was filed at the High Court over claims that Johnson & Johnson’s baby powder caused illnesses, including ovarian cancer, between 1965 and 2023.

Kenvue is fighting the claims, saying that it expects a judge to conclude its talc-based baby powder does not cause cancer.

The legal case led to claims that the scrutiny risked putting off potential buyers, with companies including Britain’s Haleon having previously been rumoured as possible buyers.

Kenvue kicked off a process to assess selling either parts or the whole company earlier this year in response to pressure from activist investors.

It ousted its chief executive in July as part of a push to prepare for a sale.

data.title: I hide money from my husband. Every partner should
data.description:
There are some secrets that should never be divulged to any human being other than your bank manager. How much you fork out on hairdressing, for example, or the exact nature of bar bills in London drinking spots. While I’m generally a stickler for honesty in married life, no relationship is improved by sharing such details. So, I wasn’t surprised to learn, courtesy of research from the Co-op’s legal services, that one in three married people aged 65 or over keep some form of financial asset hidden from their spouse, with 14 per cent silently sitting on £50,000 or more of unknown savings.

Too blooming right they do. Even those of us in our 50s can recognise the wisdom of this gambit. It’s one thing to pay your way and show largesse in wedded life (in fact, it should be obligatory), but quite another to reveal every last detail of your income and savings. There are plenty of people who appear profligate to casual observers, yet are surprisingly shrewd in establishing clandestine accounts with funds for birthday treats, holidays and egregious tax bills. (I’m speaking on behalf of a friend, you understand.) If your other half were to learn of such an arrangement, he might raid it for his own absurd priorities, like new brake pads for the car.

When I met my husband, 32 years ago, we were united in our scorn for joint bank accounts. We didn’t discuss the reasons, but I reckon both of us realised that our trivial expenditures wouldn’t stand up to rigorous scrutiny and shouldn’t be exposed to it. I didn’t want to know, or resent, how much he spent on Tamiya model Spitfires or bootleg Bob Dylan CDs, and he certainly was better off ignorant of my frock and chocolate habits. He will happily buy every Donna Leon thriller in hardback, while believing that taking a taxi, even in driving rain, is as decadent as splurging your hard-earned wedge on casinos and Playboys.

The fact is, we remain aligned on all key domestic finance-related issues and trust one another not to fritter our cash on dodgy investment schemes. Neither of us has had a credit card in three decades of married life, nor any loans outside our mortgage, as we both have a morbid fear of debt. However, that doesn’t mean we don’t regard our respective personal finances as a sacred mystery on a par with the Turin shroud.

I remain glad that I was too shamefaced to tell him when I spent a grand on a Paula Rego lithograph 24 years ago, when we were struggling to make ends meet. But then the sum my husband inherited when his parents died remains equally hazy, allowing him to continue switching off the heating in all weathers except Met-certified blizzards. I’m just grateful that the bequest financed our loft conversion, while sensing it was also linked to the sudden appearance of a Persian carpet and a Lego model of the Tower of Orthanc from The Lord of the Rings.

In similar spirit, I have a “running away” fund in an undisclosed bank account, in the unlikely event I ever need to go the full Agatha Christie and go missing for 11 days – only to be found at a hotel in Harrogate, having registered under the name of my husband’s former girlfriend. I would not begrudge my spouse his own secret stash for flights of fantasy and sanity.

Individuals in a marriage need a degree of autonomy to maintain the respect of their spouse, which in turn entails some level of financial independence. It’s a good thing to be transparent, but far nobler to whip out rainy-day savings to fix the boiler and keep the family toasty.

data.title: Britain risks losing access to the world’s best drugs, warns AstraZeneca
data.description:
NHS patients risk losing access to the world’s best medicines unless the UK spends more on developing new treatments, the head of Britain’s largest drug group has warned.

Sir Pascal Soriot, chief executive of AstraZeneca, said the NHS could be forced to rely on cheaper, older medicines unless the Government changes course and spends more on developing new treatments.

Countries including the UK risked only being “able to afford generics” in the future if they continued to squeeze spending on innovative medicines, he said.

Generics are older drugs which are less cutting-edge, but have fallen off patent and so can be made more cheaply.

Sir Pascal said Britain was “hitting the bottom” in terms of its budget for medicines.

Currently, the UK spends around 9pc of its overall healthcare budget on branded medicines. Of that, only 7pc is spent on innovative medicines. It means the UK significantly lags other G7 countries.

The UK’s expenditure on new innovative medicines currently stands at just 0.28pc of GDP – around a third of the United States’s proportionate spending of 0.78pc of GDP.

“How low can you go?” Sir Pascal said. “Technologies are going to the US and they’re going to China and other parts of the world, so in 15 or 20 years, Europe could easily lose its health sovereignty.”

It will add to pressure on the Government after a wave of drug companies recently scrapped UK investments, citing unattractive NHS clawbacks and access to patients.

AstraZeneca earlier this year paused a £200m investment that would have expanded its research and development operations in Cambridge and drug bosses have argued that the UK is now “uninvestable”.

In September, Lord Vallance, the science minister, admitted that the NHS would have to spend more on new medicines to stop a wave of investment leaving Britain.

However, reports have suggested that the Treasury is reluctant to agree to a larger overall budget, pushing for any increase to be funded out of the existing allotted departmental cash.

Sir Keir Starmer has recently intervened in the row, with plans to overhaul a decades-old formula used to determine whether medicines are deemed value for money. In theory, this could allow the NHS to buy billions of pounds more in new drugs.

However, Sir Pascal said the Government also needed to reduce the size of NHS medicine clawbacks – or drug makers would be forced to pay more in NHS rebates in response to the rising medicine spend.

He said: “Ultimately, if you get a better price and then a year later, a big [rebate] rate, it doesn’t really help anything.”

It follows mounting pressure on Labour to offer a better drug pricing deal to pharmaceutical companies.

Earlier this week, Warren Stephens, the US ambassador, warned that more drug firms “will not only cancel future investments, they will shut down their facilities in the UK” if ministers did not quickly change drug prices.

He said: “This would be a major blow to a country that prides itself, rightly so, on its life sciences sector.”

data.title: NHS to pay £3bn more for drugs to escape Trump tariffs
data.description:
The NHS has agreed to pay 25pc more for new medicines to escape Donald Trump’s sweeping new tariffs on drugs.

The US president unveiled a new pharmaceutical trade deal with Britain on Monday, striking an agreement to impose zero import tariffs on medical products from the UK.

It will see British medicines carved out from sweeping new levies, offering a major boost for one of Britain’s biggest exports to America. In 2024, the UK exported around £6.6bn in pharmaceutical and medical products to the US.

The agreement includes a pledge for the NHS to increase the net price it pays for new medicines by 25pc, which will add around £3bn to the taxpayer’s £12bn annual medical spending bill. This will be phased in.

It follows weeks of uncertainty over the future of the industry. In August, the US president threatened to impose tariffs of up to 250pc on all pharmaceutical imports – including the UK.

In September, he said there would be 100pc tariffs on “any branded or patented Pharmaceutical Product” coming into the US from October 1. At the time, he said companies could get an exemption if they had started building factories in the US.

While the White House has agreed to a spate of country-specific trade deals over the past year, pharma tariffs are expected to fall outside the scope of these agreements.

Under the new agreement, the NHS has also committed to slashing rebates that drug companies have to pay on sales of newer medicines to the health service.

The so-called Voluntary Scheme for Branded Medicines Pricing, Access and Growth (VPAG) is designed to cap the overall NHS drug bill but pharmaceutical companies have complained it has failed to keep pace with inflation.

Additionally, the deal means the UK will cut the rebate rate from 22.9pc to 15pc in 2026.

It is also understood that the Government will increase the threshold that determines which treatments are available on the NHS, boosting the spending cap on certain medicines and paving the way for more cutting-edge treatments to be introduced.

Drug giants have long complained over the decades-old formula used to determine whether medicines are deemed value for money, claiming this does not allow them to get the best new drugs into the NHS.

Jamieson Greer, the US trade representative, said: “For too long, American patients have been forced to subsidise prescription drugs and biologics in other developed countries by paying a significant premium for the same products in ours.

“Today, the United States and the United Kingdom announce this negotiated outcome pricing for innovative pharmaceuticals, which will help drive investment and innovation in both countries.”

He added: “The Trump Administration is reviewing the pharmaceutical pricing practices of many other US trading partners and hopes that they will follow suit with constructive negotiations.”

Howard Lutnick, the US commerce secretary, said the deal would bring new jobs to America.

Mr Lutnick said: “This deal doesn’t just deepen our economic partnership with the United Kingdom – it ensures that the breakthroughs of tomorrow will be built, tested and produced on American soil.”

The White House has previously made clear its frustration over the lack of spending on innovative medicines in Britain. Currently, the UK spends just 0.28pc of its GDP on new medicines, roughly a third of what America does.

Britain previously pledged to review the health service’s drug pricing to take into account “the concerns of the president” under the UK-US trade deal struck in May.

Mr Trump has called for an end to overseas countries “freeloading” on American innovation, singling out Britain for a place where medicine prices are unfairly low compared to the US.

The US ambassador last month said Britain needed to change how it priced medicines or risk more global drug companies scrapping investments in the UK.

Merck, one of America’s largest drug companies, this year ditched plans to open a £1bn research centre in London, while AstraZeneca has paused £200m spending on its Cambridge research facility.

Liz Kendall, the Science and Technology Secretary, said the deal would “enable and incentivise life sciences companies to continue to invest and innovate right here in the UK”.

Chris Boerner, chief executive of Bristol Myers Squibb, said the trade agreement meant the pharmaceutical company will invest “upwards of $500m (£378m)” over the next five years.

Mr Boerner said: “This investment will come across multiple areas of the business including research, development and manufacturing. This agreement is a sign of progress and one that creates an environment conducive to our continued presence in the UK.”
